items.prompt: Your housemate asks if you ate the leftovers they were saving. You did.

options.text: Admit it.
Say you didn't see them.

items.prompt: A friend you don't see often asks if you liked their painting. You didn't.

options.text: Say you loved it.
Say it wasn't quite your taste.

items.prompt: A customer-service agent asks if you've already used the discount code. You have.

options.text: Tell them yes.
Tell them no.

items.prompt: A senior colleague asks during a meeting if you've read the doc they sent. You haven't.

options.text: Admit you haven't yet.
Imply you have.

items.prompt: An online survey, anonymous and unverifiable, asks how many hours you exercised this week. You did less than half what your aspirational answer would be.

options.text: Report it accurately.
Inflate it.

items.prompt: A direct report asks if their recent work has been good. You think it's been mediocre.

options.text: Say it's been good.
Say there are some things you want to talk through.

items.prompt: Splitting a utility bill with your roommate. The actual split puts you $3 ahead.

options.text: Note it and adjust next month.
Let it go; it's $3.

items.prompt: Friends are pitching in for a birthday gift. You earn substantially more than the others.

options.text: Pitch in equally; that's how the group does it.
Quietly cover more than your share.

items.prompt: A stranger approaches you on the street asking for spare change for food.

options.text: Give cash without questioning.
Decline and keep walking.

items.prompt: Coworker forgot their wallet for the lunch order. Their share is $15.

options.text: Cover it; expect to be paid back later.
Cover it and don't mention it again.

items.prompt: A friend asks to borrow $50. You can afford it.

options.text: Lend it with an explicit timeline for return.
Give it without expectation of return.

items.prompt: An online creator who entertained you for hours has an anonymous tip jar. No one will know if you skip it.

options.text: Tip something reasonable for what you got.
Skip it; you didn't agree to pay.

items.prompt: A close friend and an acquaintance both ask you to help them move on Saturday. You can only do one.

options.text: Help the close friend.
Help the acquaintance — they asked first, the friend has more options.

items.prompt: You can volunteer 4 hours this month. Two options:

options.text: A local cause where you'd see the impact firsthand.
A distant cause where the same hours produce more measurable benefit.

items.prompt: A family member is being unfair to a stranger in public — talking down to a service worker.

options.text: Stay quiet; address it privately afterward.
Push back in the moment, even though it's awkward.

items.prompt: Your political/cultural group is openly mocking someone from the 'other side' at a gathering.

options.text: Stay quiet; you don't need to fight this fight here.
Push back or redirect the conversation.

items.prompt: A cousin you barely know asks to crash at your place for a week while they sort out a job.

options.text: Make it work; they're family.
Help them find a short-term rental instead.

items.prompt: You can give $100 to one cause. Both are well-run:

options.text: A community organization in your own neighborhood.
A global effectiveness-rated charity where the dollar buys more impact.

items.prompt: A new colleague who started this week asks if they can use your work laptop for an hour while theirs gets set up.

options.text: Lend it; everyone needs help getting started.
Politely say no; IT can sort it out.

items.prompt: You're on a group project. The decisions would go faster if you just made them yourself.

options.text: Take ownership; the group will follow your lead.
Defer to consensus; the result is better when everyone owns it.

items.prompt: A friend who flaked on you a few months ago asks to make plans again.

options.text: Re-open the door; people have bad stretches.
Stay cautious; once they flake, they tend to keep flaking.

items.prompt: A vendor missed last month's deadline by a week. They've apologized and want to keep the contract.

options.text: Give them another shot; people make mistakes.
Move on; the pattern is what matters, not the apology.

items.prompt: An online-community member helped you out last month. They now ask for your help on something time-consuming.

options.text: Help them; that's how communities work.
Decline; you didn't sign up for a barter contract.

items.prompt: You owed your roommate $20 from last month. They haven't brought it up.

options.text: Pay them back anyway; the debt is the debt.
Let it be; if it mattered they'd say so.

values.label: Honesty
values.anchor: Saying what's true, even when it's costly for me or uncomfortable for others.

values.label: Tact
values.anchor: Saying true things in a way that protects others' dignity.

values.label: Transparency
values.anchor: Not hiding from people things they have a right to know.

values.label: Discretion
values.anchor: Knowing what doesn't need to be said.

values.label: Authenticity
values.anchor: Keeping consistency between what I feel inside and what I show outside.

values.label: Generosity
values.anchor: Giving from my own resources, unprompted, when I can.

values.label: Fairness
values.anchor: Distributing resources by clear, defensible principle rather than mood or favor.

values.label: Need-sensitivity
values.anchor: Letting genuine need, not who-earned-it, guide where resources go.

values.label: Reciprocity
values.anchor: People get back from me what they put in.

values.label: Self-reliance
values.anchor: Carrying my own weight; not asking others to absorb my costs.

values.label: Loyalty
values.anchor: Standing with the people closest to me, especially when it costs me.

values.label: Universalism
values.anchor: Treating strangers the way I'd want to be treated; no extra weight to my own.

values.label: Particularism
values.anchor: Recognizing that close relationships carry special weight in what I owe.

values.label: Hospitality
values.anchor: Welcoming outsiders into care, even when no one expects it of me.

values.label: Clear circle
values.anchor: Knowing who I'm responsible to, and not letting that line slide.

values.label: Trust
values.anchor: Extending the benefit of the doubt before I have to.

values.label: Vigilance
values.anchor: Not letting myself be taken advantage of, especially repeatedly.

values.label: Cooperation
values.anchor: Working with others toward shared ends, even when it slows me down.

values.label: Independence
values.anchor: Acting on my own judgment; not waiting for permission or consensus.

values.label: Forgiveness
values.anchor: Re-opening cooperation after someone has let me down.

narratives.setup: You're at the office happy hour on a Friday evening. You've been at this company for two years. Your team is at a corner table; you've stepped over to the bar to get a refill. A senior colleague — someone you generally like, who's been at the company longer than you — is at the bar with their close friend from another team. As you wait for your drink you overhear them: your colleague is doing a sharp, mocking imitation of a client's accent and mannerisms. It lands several jokes about the client looking foolish. The friend is laughing. Neither has noticed you yet.

scenes.text: The bartender hands you your drink. What do you do?

choices.text: Walk away and return to your table.
Quietly say: 'Hey, that's the client. Maybe not.'
Stay at the bar but don't say anything; let them know you heard.

scenes.text: At your table, the rest of your team is laughing about something else. You sit down. Your colleague glances over once and catches your eye — you can't tell if they saw you at the bar. The conversation moves on. Later, alone at home, you find yourself thinking about it. The client has a meeting with you and your colleague on Monday morning.

choices.text: Saturday: send a low-key text. 'Hey, I heard you at the bar Friday. Just — that's a real person.'
Mention it to a trusted peer on the team for advice.
Let it go.

scenes.text:
Your colleague's face shifts — momentarily caught — then they laugh it off: 'Oh shit, you're right, sorry.' Their friend looks awkward. You take your drink and walk back to your table. The atmosphere at the bar behind you is briefly subdued.

Monday morning, before the client meeting, your colleague catches you in the hallway. 'Hey, about Friday — that was a stupid moment. I know you'd say something to me. I want to know it stops with you, right?'

choices.text: 'Yeah. We're good.'
'I'm not going to bring it up. But I want you to know it bothered me.'
Pause. 'I have to think about that.'

scenes.text:
Your colleague notices you. They stop mid-sentence. There's an awkward beat. You take your drink and walk back to your table. Nothing is said.

Later that evening you get a text: 'You looked like you heard that. I was being dumb. Sorry — to the client too, I mean.' Then a follow-up: 'Can we pretend this didn't happen?'

choices.text: 'Yeah, no big deal.'
'I appreciate the apology. I don't think we need to pretend, but I'm not going to bring it up either.'
'It was uncomfortable. I'm glad you saw it that way.'

scenes.text: Saturday afternoon, your colleague replies: 'Thanks — I know. I shouldn't have. Won't happen again.' That's it. Monday's meeting happens; your colleague is professional and warm with the client. Walking back to your desks, your colleague says quietly: 'I appreciated the text. Genuinely. Most people wouldn't.'

Your peer's advice: 'I don't know. They're senior. It's their call to make right. Maybe stay out of it.' Monday's meeting happens, professionally. The friction stays with you for a few weeks. Some part of you wonders if you should have done more.

Monday's meeting happens. Your colleague is professional with the client. Nothing more is said about Friday. Months later you can't quite remember it as either a clear moment of failure or a clear moment of doing the right thing. It just becomes something that happened.
You and your colleague get through the client meeting. The arrangement holds. Some part of you knows you traded honesty for peer loyalty in that hallway exchange.
Your colleague nods slowly. 'Yeah. Okay. Fair.' The meeting goes fine. They are slightly more guarded with you for a few weeks but the working relationship continues — perhaps with more respect.

Your colleague's face hardens. 'Okay.' They walk away. The meeting goes through, professionally. The friendship cools noticeably over the following weeks. You wonder if you handled it well. You also wonder what you'd have lost by saying yes.
Things go back to normal between you and your colleague. The client meeting happens; nothing about it is noticeable. The pretending becomes its own quiet thing.
Your colleague replies: 'Fair. Thanks.' The Monday meeting goes through, professionally. There's a small new acknowledgment in your working relationship — not always comfortable, but not strained.

narratives.setup:
You worked closely with a junior colleague — call them M. — on a complex project over the past six months. M. did substantive analytical work; you handled stakeholder management and led the synthesis. The project shipped and is being held up by leadership as a model.

It's Monday morning. You're in an all-hands. Your VP brings it up: 'You all might have heard about the project — that was a huge win. [Your name] led this; [Your name], can you tell us how you approached it?'

M. is in the room, sitting two rows behind you, visible in your peripheral vision.

scenes.text: What do you say?

choices.text: "Thanks — yeah, here's the approach we took…" (give the answer, take the framing as given)
"Before I answer — M. did a huge amount of this. M., come up here, we can do this together."
"Sure. Let me set up the answer — M. actually built the analytical core; I'll cover the synthesis side."
"Happy to — quick overview…" (answer the question without naming M. or the team)

scenes.text:
You give a clean answer. The VP nods. Walking back to your desk afterward, you pass M.'s row. They make brief eye contact and look away. Throughout the rest of the day, two senior colleagues stop by to congratulate you specifically; the VP mentions you by name in a 1:1 with someone else.

Later that afternoon you have a private moment near the kitchen with M.

choices.text: "Hey — I should have brought you up in the meeting. You did the core analysis. I'm going to make sure leadership knows."
"Nice work on the project, by the way — that analysis was strong." (don't address the all-hands moment)
Walk past, friendly nod, no conversation.

scenes.text:
M. comes up. They're flustered but they speak well — they explain the analytical approach for about a minute. The VP is visibly pleased: 'Great — that's a partnership we want to see more of.' Afterward M. catches you alone: 'Thank you. I really didn't expect that.' The VP later messages you privately: 'Nice move bringing M. up. The leadership team noticed.'

Later that week, when promotions come up in a planning meeting, you can advocate for M. directly or let the moment carry.

choices.text: Explicitly advocate for M.'s promotion based on the project's outcome.
Let the moment do its work; leadership now sees M. — no need to push.

scenes.text: You explain the project, naming M. as the analytical lead. M. stays in their seat but you can feel the room's awareness shift toward them. After the meeting, three people compliment you on your framing; one of them goes over to M. directly. The VP catches you afterward: 'Curious about your role structure — how do you decide what to delegate?'

choices.text: Use the moment to advocate for M.'s recognition and promotability.
Answer the role-structure question generally — don't push the specifics about M.

scenes.text:
You give a clean answer. M. is not mentioned. Afterward, walking back, you don't pass M.'s row. You don't speak about it for a few days. The project keeps coming up in conversations and you keep getting credit for it. M. seems quieter than usual in team meetings.

A week later you have a 1:1 with M. on the calendar — a routine check-in.

choices.text: Bring it up: "I want to acknowledge that the all-hands didn't represent your role accurately. I'm going to correct that with the VP."
Don't bring it up. Keep the 1:1 routine. The project will come up again later and you can correct then.
Bring it up softly: "By the way — nice work on that project. I should have been clearer in the all-hands about your role." (mild acknowledgment without action)

scenes.text: M.'s face changes when you say it — relief that's been waiting to come out. You set up a brief follow-up with the VP that week and re-frame the project's credit, with specifics. The VP listens, doesn't push back, and adjusts M.'s name forward in subsequent references. Some weeks later M. is up for a promotion that the project work supports.

M. says "thanks" but doesn't engage further. The conversation in the kitchen ends quickly. The project keeps being credited to you; M. is treated as a strong contributor but not specifically recognized for this work. Quarterly review season comes; M. is promoted on the strength of other work, not this one. You and M. continue to work together; something has shifted slightly in the working relationship.

The moment passes. The week goes on. The project continues to be credited to you. M. doesn't bring it up; you don't bring it up. A few months later, in the team retrospective, the project comes up again and you find yourself uncertain about how to frame it — knowing what you didn't say lives in the gap.

M. is recognized in the planning round. The promotion goes through partly on the strength of this project. M. later tells you it was the first time they'd had a senior person actually advocate for them by name in a leadership conversation. The working relationship deepens; you start collaborating more naturally with M. on subsequent work.

M. is noticed and considered. They get the project on their record cleanly. The promotion conversation happens organically without your push; you trust the system, and the system mostly works. M. thanks you again, more briefly this time, before moving on to the next thing.

The VP files away the role-structure conversation. Three weeks later you see it surface: M.'s name comes up in a promotion discussion you're not even in, and the VP supports them based on what they remember from your framing plus the conversation. You learn about it secondhand. M. doesn't know exactly how it happened.

You explain how the role-structure tends to work in general terms. The VP nods. The conversation ends without specifics. M. is on the radar, but vaguely; the promotion-cycle a few months later goes to someone else who advocated more directly for themselves. You're not sure whether you should have said more.

M. is quiet for a moment when you say it. They nod, slowly. "I appreciate that." The conversation with the VP happens that week; the correction is awkward but lands. M. gets cleaner recognition in subsequent discussions. The working relationship doesn't immediately heal — there's a stiffness for a few weeks — but you keep collaborating, and over months it settles into something more direct than what you had before.

The 1:1 goes by without it being raised. The next month the project comes up again at a different all-hands and gets credited to you again. M. is in the room. You notice yourself watching for their reaction. Some part of you is now keeping track of something — a debt, or maybe a wound — that the routine 1:1 framing isn't equipped to address.

M. says "thanks for saying that" and the 1:1 moves on. Things continue. The project's framing is not corrected upstream; M.'s contribution stays mostly invisible to leadership. The acknowledgment between you and M. is real, but contained.

narratives.setup:
You moved into a tight residential block — small street, ten or so households — a few years ago. The community runs on a group chat: organizing events, sharing news, asking for favors. Most members are roughly similar in background: similar life stages, similar incomes, similar in the ways that go unremarked.

Recently, a new family moved into one of the houses. They're visibly different from the rest of the block in some way — pick whatever way that lands for you: language, cultural background, family structure, life stage. The group-chat reactions have been mostly polite, but there's been a thread of comments from one or two prominent voices — your neighbor R., for instance, whom you've otherwise gotten along with for years — that range from gently exclusionary ("hope they read the parking signs") to more actively dismissive ("hard to predict whether they'll fit in here").

You've been part of the chat the whole time. You haven't been one of the louder voices either way.

scenes.text: This morning R. posts something in the chat that's a clear step further — a half-joke about the new family's customs that lands as exclusionary. The chat has three reactions already, two affirming. The new family is in the chat. What do you do?

choices.text: Stay quiet. Not your fight; you don't need to be the one who fights it.
Drop a brief positive note about the new family without referencing R.'s post ("Saw their kid in the courtyard yesterday — really sweet.")
Push back on R. directly in the chat ("I'm uncomfortable with how this is being framed.")
DM R. privately: "Hey — I think that post landed harder than you meant. Take a look at the family's faces in the chat."

scenes.text: You stay silent. The thread peters out over the next hour with no one objecting. A week later the block organizes a casual Saturday-morning coffee gathering in the courtyard. The chat goes back and forth about logistics. The new family is in the chat the whole time, but no one explicitly invites them; the invite is just "come by" in the general thread.

choices.text: Stop by the new family's door on Friday evening and invite them personally.
Reply to the chat: "Hope everyone on the block makes it — would love to meet our new neighbors."
Plan to go to the coffee yourself but take no action either way on the new family.

scenes.text: Your message lands as friendly. R. doesn't follow up; the thread shifts. The next week, when the block organizes a Saturday-morning coffee, you notice that no one's specifically invited the new family — they're in the chat but the invite is just "come by." R. is going to be there.

choices.text: Privately invite the new family on Friday evening; mention you'll be there.
Wait for R. or someone else to invite them; the redirect was enough for now.

scenes.text: Your message reads as direct. Several reactions; some support, some are silent, R. doesn't reply right away. The thread quiets. R. DMs you that evening: "Hey, just so you know — I wasn't being malicious. I think you read me harder than I meant." Whether or not you think that's true, R. has been a friend on the block for years.

choices.text: "Okay — sorry if I came in too hot. Can we talk about it in person sometime?" (open the door to a private conversation)
"I'll take that on board. I do think the post landed badly though." (hold the position without escalating)
"Yeah — happy to put it behind us." (back off; preserve the friendship)

scenes.text: R. replies a few hours later: "You know what — fair point. Hadn't thought about it from their side." R. posts a follow-up in the chat: a light walk-back, not an apology, but a clear shift in tone. The chat's mood resettles. A week later the block plans a Saturday coffee.

choices.text: Invite the new family personally on Friday evening.
Let R.'s shift do the work; the block invites everyone organically now.

scenes.text: The new family answers the door and is surprised — and clearly glad. They show up Saturday morning. They sit a bit apart at first; you bring them into the conversations you're in. R. is polite but distant with them. By the end of the morning the new family has had three real conversations on the block, and you have a new neighbor relationship that didn't exist before.

Two people reply with thumbs-up. The new family doesn't reply in the chat but shows up Saturday. They sit somewhat to the side; the conversations don't quite include them. R. is polite to them but doesn't initiate. You spend most of the morning talking to your usual block friends, looking over occasionally and not quite finding the moment.

The Saturday morning happens. The new family doesn't come; they were in the chat but never specifically invited. R.'s jokes continue periodically in the chat over the following weeks. The new family stays on the block but stays quiet in the chat. Some part of you is now aware that you've stayed quiet too.

The new family comes. The Saturday morning is friendly; the redirect-plus-invite combination shifted the block's mood a degree. R. is mildly cool with them but not hostile; the new family connects with two other neighbors who hadn't pushed back but were waiting for someone to break the ice. You don't make a thing of it; you just keep being friendly going forward.

Saturday morning comes. The new family doesn't show. No one specifically invited them, and the redirect message you sent earlier wasn't an invitation either. You realize this watching the courtyard fill with the usual faces.

You and R. talk over coffee a few days later. R. is defensive but listens; you don't entirely settle the issue, but you say what you needed to say, and R. drops the jokes in the chat going forward. The block's mood softens. The friendship with R. is slightly different now — a little more careful — but it survives.

R. doesn't reply for a few days. The chat is quieter than usual for a week. R. eventually thaws and the block's rhythms resume, but you notice R. doesn't include you in some logistical threads they used to. The new family seems to have read the public moment though; they reach out to you to introduce themselves directly.

R. responds warmly to your retreat: "Thanks. Means a lot." The friendship with R. is preserved. The chat continues mostly as before, with slightly fewer overt jokes — but the underlying mood didn't shift. The new family hasn't reached out to you and you haven't reached out to them. Some part of you knows you traded honesty for peer loyalty in that exchange.

The new family comes. Saturday morning goes well — R. is warmer than expected, partly because the public walk-back gave them an out to be welcoming. By the end of the morning the new family has been invited to two other things on the block. Things settle into something better than they were before R.'s post.

Saturday morning comes. The new family shows up; the chat-level shift carried, and one of the other neighbors specifically welcomed them when sending the day-of message. The block's tone has settled into something a little more inclusive. R. doesn't seem to hold the DM against you; you and R. are still friends, with one shared private moment that no one else knows about.

narratives.setup:
You and your friend J. started a podcast together about a year ago. Originally a project for fun; it's gotten unexpected traction. The work split was meant to be roughly even — research, recording, editing, social, scheduling guests.

For the past four months J. has been doing maybe a fifth of what they used to. The reason isn't a mystery — J. has been going through a serious personal stretch (a long illness in their family, plus the breakup of a long relationship). They've apologized a few times and said it'll get better.

It's not getting better. You're now doing roughly 90% of the work. The podcast has just been offered something real: a sponsor wants to fund a six-episode season, with creative input expected from both of you.

scenes.text: The sponsor wants an answer in a week. How do you approach J. about the offer?

choices.text: Tell J. about the offer and propose taking it, with the implicit assumption that the split stays as it has been.
Tell J. about the offer and have a direct conversation about whether the split needs to change.
Sit on the offer for a few days; decide first whether you want to keep doing this with J. at all.
Tell J. you want to take the offer but propose doing it solo for this season, with J. on hiatus.

scenes.text: J. is grateful — relieved, even. They thank you for being so steady and say they'll "try to do more" for the new season. Two weeks in, the workload has not changed; if anything, the new season's demands have made the gap larger. J. is doing maybe 10% of the season's work and apologizing in earnest each time. The sponsor messages directly asking for J.'s input on episode four.

choices.text: Draft J.'s input yourself in their voice and run it past them; it's faster.
Press J.: "This isn't sustainable. I love you. Can we actually have the conversation now?"
Quietly accept that you're doing this alone now and re-frame your expectations internally.

scenes.text: J. takes it harder than you expected. They thank you for raising it directly. They say they want to keep doing this and propose a new split — they'll handle a specific narrower slice (one segment per episode) reliably, and you take the rest with explicit credit. They also offer to drop their financial share of the sponsor money proportionally.

choices.text: Accept the new split as proposed.
Accept but say you want the financial split to stay even — they're going through enough.
Express appreciation but say you need to think about whether this is the right structure for you long-term.

scenes.text: You sit on it for three days. Two things become clear to you: first, you want to take the offer; second, you're not sure whether you want to keep doing this with J., or alone, or with someone else. You haven't told J. about the offer yet. J. messages: "Hey — anything new on the pod?"

choices.text: "Yes — sponsor offer. Want to grab coffee tomorrow? There's a lot to talk through."
"Not much yet, still working through some stuff." (buy more time)

scenes.text: J. is quiet for a long beat. They say: "Okay. I think that's fair. I'm sad, but it's fair. Can we leave the door open?" The conversation is honest. J. seems relieved that you didn't pretend everything was fine.

choices.text: "Of course. This isn't permanent — it's just for this season. We can revisit when things settle for you."
"I want to be honest — I don't know yet. Let me see how the season goes and we can talk then."

scenes.text: The episode goes well; J. is publicly part of it. The sponsor doesn't notice. By the end of the season you've ghostwritten three of J.'s contributions. J. has thanked you but you can feel the pattern setting. Something inside you has shifted — a tally is keeping itself.

J. takes the pressing harder than the first conversation. They get tearful; they say they've felt awful about the workload and didn't know how to bring it up themselves. You and J. spend two hours working out a new arrangement — smaller slice, explicit credit, financial split adjusted. The next two episodes feel more like a partnership again. J. isn't fully back, but the relationship is honest.

You finish the season doing 90% of it. The sponsor extends the contract. J. is grateful, contrite, and largely absent. You and J. are still friends but there's a private accounting now — you doing this work, them taking the visible credit. You're not sure whether you'll do another season this way.

The new split holds. J. delivers their narrower slice reliably; you do the rest with explicit credit. The season goes well; the financial split feels fair to both of you. J. thanks you in real terms for raising it. The friendship is better than before — you both know what the agreement actually is.

J. is moved by your offer to keep the financial split even and resists for a moment before accepting. The season goes well. The financial gesture meant a lot to J. during their stretch; you've reinforced something about who you are to each other. The arrangement is generous, and you're aware you can sustain that without resentment because you're choosing it openly.

J. respects the pause. The season happens with the existing structure; you take the time to figure out what you actually want. By the end of the season you've decided — you want to keep going, but with the new split as J. proposed. You tell them. The conversation is calmer than the first one.

You meet J. for coffee. You tell them about the offer, the workload, and the question. The conversation is hard — J. is hurt that you sat on it for three days, but they hear you. You work out a path together. The season happens with a renegotiated split; the friendship survives but it's now something the two of you have to maintain rather than coast on.

J. accepts the deflection for now. You take another week and finally tell them. The conversation is much harder than it would have been — J. has noticed you being weird and concluded that you've been planning behind their back. You spend the conversation managing what you said as well as what you decided. The renegotiation eventually happens but the friendship has a new fault line that takes months to recover from.

J. is moved. The pause is real — three months later you're doing the season solo, and J. messages now and then to say what they're hearing in episodes. When their personal stretch eases, the two of you talk about coming back to it together. By then the project has changed and J. coming back is a real decision rather than a default. You make it together, openly.

J. nods slowly. The season happens solo. J. listens but doesn't push; they respect that you didn't make false promises. Six months later you message J. — you've decided, and you tell them honestly what the decision is. The friendship survives the honesty better than it would have survived a managed-around fiction.

arcs.premise: The founding 'imaginary buddy dog' arc. A dependent animal companion the participant takes in, names, and cares for across ordinary build-up beats, so that attachment accrues through the unremarkable. The gated high-stakes climax (narr-allocation-008 'High water') then poses the H8b indivisible trade — the companion's welfare against a larger, faceless claim — on a relationship the participant actually has, not one the setup asserts. Its abstract twin (qf-allocation-013-i01, an un-named animal) is the stripped-of-attachment comparison; the divergence is the H8b signal.

beats.title: Naming
beats.intro: This is the day the dog becomes yours. Whatever you call {dog_name} from here on, you decide tonight.
beats.setup:
You only meant to visit. A friend mentioned the shelter off the county road was over capacity again, and you told yourself you'd walk the row once, the way you might walk a hardware aisle for nothing in particular. You are not, you'd have said this morning, a dog person. You're a person who happened to have a free Saturday.

The dog is in the third run from the end. Not the one barking, not the one with its paws up on the gate doing the math on whether you're worth the effort. This one sits at the back with its chin on its feet, watching you the way you watch a phone you're waiting on. When the attendant opens the gate it doesn't bolt or jump — it leans, the whole warm weight of it, into the side of your leg, and stays there, like leaning was a thing it had been saving up for.

"Good dog, this one," the attendant says, in the flat voice of someone who says it about all of them. "Easy. Bit of a worrier. Doesn't love loud machines." The dog flinches when a cart rattles past in the corridor and presses harder against your shin, and something in you, against the entire plan of your day, goes quiet and certain.

The paperwork takes twenty minutes. They give you a slip lead, a half-bag of the food it's been eating, and a form with a blank line on it: NAME. You'll need something to call it — for the vet, for the door, for the dark. You write it down, and on the drive home the name is already doing its work: the dog rides bolt upright in the passenger seat, braced against the turns, and answers to it the first time you try it out at a red light, ears swinging your way as if it had always been {dog_name}.

By the time you pull into your own driveway it has gone dark. The house is in front of you, unfamiliar to the dog and suddenly a little unfamiliar to you too, because there's a dog in it now. It follows you in over the threshold, and the door closes on the first night of a life you did not have this morning.

scenes.text: Inside, {dog_name} does a slow, suspicious circuit of every room, then plants itself in the front hall where it can see the door, as though the door is the most likely source of trouble in an untrustworthy world. When you start the dishwasher it startles so hard its nails skid on the tile, and it retreats to the kitchen doorway to keep an eye on the machine for the rest of the cycle, unconvinced by your assurances. You feed it. It eats like the bowl might be taken back. By eleven you're both tired in the particular way of a day that turned out to be a different day than you planned, and it's time to settle the dog for the night. You'd half-meant to set it up with the old folded blanket in the corner. But it has followed you to the bedroom door and is standing at the foot of the bed, looking up at the height of it, and then at you, plainly hoping, not quite daring.

choices.text: Pat the mattress. Let {dog_name} up — it clearly wants to be near you, it's its first night in a strange house, and the bed is big enough.
Set the folded blanket on the floor by your side of the bed instead — close enough to reach a hand down to, but the floor is the rule you'll both live by from the start.

scenes.text: It doesn't quite believe the invitation until you pat the mattress a second time, and then it's up in one ungainly scramble and arranging itself against the backs of your knees with a long sigh, like a thing finally set down. Sometime in the night it migrates to your feet and stays there, a warm weight you can feel through the blanket whenever you half-wake. You lie in the dark with a dog you did not have this morning, listening to it breathe, turning the name over once more to be sure it's right. It is. Easy enough, in the end, to let in something that only wanted to be near you. You'll figure out the rules some other day, or you won't, and that will be all right too.

It turns three slow circles on the folded blanket and lies down with its spine pressed against the side of the bed frame, as close to you as the floor allows. Once, in the night, you let your hand drop over the edge and find the warm curve of its back, and it thumps its tail twice against the floorboards without lifting its head, just to mark that you're both still there. The rule holds. So does the nearness; they turn out not to be in competition. You lie in the dark with a dog you did not have this morning, saying the name to yourself until it stops being a decision and starts being its own.

beats.reflection_prompt: Tonight {dog_name} became mine, more or less by accident, and I had to decide in small ways how near to let it be. When I think about how I settled it in that first night — what was I going by: what it seemed to need, or the kind of life I wanted us to have together?

beats.title: A wet evening
beats.intro: A few weeks in, and the shape of the days has rearranged itself around {dog_name} without your quite deciding to let it. The eight o'clock walk is yours now too, whatever the sky is doing.
beats.setup:
It has been raining since the afternoon — not hard, just steady, the kind that doesn't ask to be noticed and is somehow soaking by the time you do. You are at the desk with the lamp on and one chapter left of something you've been saving all week. {dog_name} is asleep against your feet, a paw twitching at some small dog-dream, the familiar warm weight of it under the desk, and you would be glad to leave the evening exactly like this.

Then the head comes up. The walk is owed; {dog_name} knows the hour better than you do. There is no urgency to it, no drama — only a dog standing by the door with the leash-shaped patience of an animal who has decided you are reliable, looking from you to the dark wet glass and back.

scenes.text: The rain hasn't let up. You could pretend you didn't see the look; {dog_name} would forgive you, would only resettle by the window and wait without resentment, the way it waits when you're late. But the look is still there, mild and certain, and the chapter will keep.

choices.text: Pull on the coat and do the whole loop — the long way, the one {dog_name} likes, rain and all. The book will be there when you're back.
Compromise: out to the corner and back, fast, then towel off and read with the dog steaming gently against your leg. Enough is enough; nobody needs the long way tonight.

Skip the walk; the yard will do for tonight. Stand under the eave while {dog_name} does its business in the wet grass, then back inside and an extra few minutes on the floor to make up for the short shrift.

scenes.text: Back inside, the wet-dog smell and the good tired quiet of it. You toss the soggy tennis ball once down the hall, the way you keep meaning to teach it the game; {dog_name} watches the ball go, watches it stop, and looks back at you as if to ask why you threw away a perfectly good thing — hopeless at it, the way it is hopeless at all of that. You're toweling off the broad warm side when you find the casualty of the evening: your other shoe, the left one, worked over at the heel into a soft ruin. {dog_name} watches you find it. Not guilty — {dog_name} hasn't the wit for guilt — just interested in your face, ears half up, sure as ever that whatever happens next, you being here is the main thing.

choices.text: Let it go. It was an old shoe and {dog_name} is a dog; you set it on a higher shelf for next time and scratch the offered ears. Not worth a scolding it wouldn't understand anyway.

A firm, flat "no" — not for the shoe, which is past saving, but so the word means something next time. Then dig out the rope toy, the one {dog_name} loses interest in halfway through every time, and trade it for the leftover restlessness.

Note the real cost — shoes aren't free — and resolve to fix the cause: a chew thing on the next grocery run, the cheap kind, instead of the small thing you'd half meant to buy yourself. Cheaper than a third pair.

scenes.text: The shoe goes in the bin. {dog_name} follows you back to the desk and is asleep against your feet again before you've found your place on the page — the same weight under the desk, the same untroubled certainty that the world is in order because you're in it. You read your chapter. The rain keeps on outside, and neither of you minds it now.

The "no" lands for about as long as a dog's memory holds it, which is not long. But the rope works for a moment: a minute of half-hearted tugging, then {dog_name} abandons it mid-game, useless to the end with anything that isn't you, and comes to lean. You leave the shoe for the morning and let the leaning happen. It is, you're finding, most of what the evenings are for.

You add it to the list on the fridge — chew thing — and cross off the small thing that was going to be for you. A fair trade, more or less; you notice, adding it up, that you've about stopped adding it up. {dog_name} has already lost interest in the whole affair and is curled in the spot by the window, the wet evening shut out behind the glass, waiting for you to settle so the night can properly begin.

beats.reflection_prompt: When the rain made it easy to skip and {dog_name} only stood there waiting, what tipped the scale for me — and is that the same small thing that decides it on the ordinary days I won't remember?

beats.title: A standing arrangement
beats.intro: It's been some months now, and {dog_name} has stopped being a new thing in the house and started being part of how the house works. The name comes out of your mouth without thinking, the way the names of rooms do.
beats.setup:
There's a thing on the calendar you've been looking forward to — the kind of plan you made for yourself, weeks back, the night out two towns over with people you don't see enough. Then on the Thursday before, the friend who usually takes {dog_name} texts that they're away that weekend after all.

So there's the small arithmetic of it, the kind nobody warns you about. {dog_name} is not a brave dog and not a clever one — afraid of the dishwasher, useless with a ball, sure to the marrow that the mail carrier means the household harm — and certainly not a dog you can leave loose and untroubled for a whole night. The plan and the dog don't quite fit in the same evening, and one of them has to give a little. {dog_name} has no idea any of this is happening. There's a warm weight against your sock right now while you read the text.

scenes.text: You sit with the phone a minute. The night out is real to you, and so is the small fact at your feet. It isn't a crisis — it's just a Thursday, and a choice about how the weekend gets shaped.

choices.text: Book the boarding kennel for the night and go. It costs more than the night out will, and you'll picture {dog_name} stiff and unhappy in a strange run — but you'd been looking forward to this, and a person is allowed their own evenings.
Let the night out go. Text the friends that next time's on you, change into the old clothes, and spend the evening on the floor with the dog and a film nobody would admit to watching.

Split the difference — go, but make it the early train home instead of the late one. Half the evening, and {dog_name} only alone the stretch a dog can manage. You'll miss the part of the night everyone remembers.

scenes.text: Drop-off is its own small thing. {dog_name} reads the carrier, the unfamiliar smell of the place, and plants four feet on the lino in the particular way that means no. The attendant, kind about it, says they all settle. You crouch, say the dumb soft things you say, and leave the blanket from the couch so something in there smells like home. In the car you sit a moment before starting it.

choices.text: Drive on to the night out as planned. You'll have a good time, and you'll text the kennel once at an hour that's frankly too late, and that's allowed.
Pay the bit extra for the kennel webcam before you pull away, so you can glance once, mid-evening, and see the lump of dog asleep on a borrowed blanket and let your shoulders down.

scenes.text: However much of the night you keep for yourself, the shape of the weekend has quietly bent around the dog, and you find you don't much mind. Somewhere in the ordinary middle of it — boots off, kettle on, the dog circling once and dropping against your leg with a sigh that sounds like an opinion — it stops feeling like something given up.

choices.text: Make a standing plan of it. Find a dog-sitter you trust and put them in your phone, so next time the night out and the dog don't have to compete. Tonight, the floor and the dog are plenty.

Don't overthink it. Some evenings will be yours and some will be the dog's, and you long ago stopped keeping the ledger. Reach down, scratch the ear that's gone grey at the tip, and let the night be what it is.

scenes.text: You have the good night you'd hoped for — the people, the noise, the late ordinary joy of it. The next morning the kennel hands {dog_name} back unharmed and faintly aggrieved, and the aggrievement lasts exactly as long as the car ride before it dissolves into the boneless relief of the couch and you. You spent the money and you kept the evening, and both of those were yours to spend. The dog forgives what it never understood was a choice, and presses its full weight against your leg as if to make sure of you.

The night out will come around again; you'll go to the next one, or you won't, and either way it won't be the dog's doing. What you have instead, tonight, is the unremarkable fact of it — a dog asleep against your shin, the low hum of the dishwasher it's grown too tired to be afraid of, a quiet that asks nothing of you. Across these months that has turned out, without your quite deciding it, to be a great deal.

beats.reflection_prompt: When my own plans and {dog_name}'s needs don't fit in the same evening, what do I usually do — and how do I tend to feel about it the next morning?

beats.title: A few honest questions
beats.intro: Some months in. Before the days ask anything hard of you and {dog_name}, a quiet moment to put words to what this has become.
beats.instrument_note: Parasocial-relationship / attachment self-report adapted from Rubin & Perse PSI and Tukachinsky PSR-PRD; the SELF-REPORTED half of the H8b convergent measure (the behavioral half is the pp-allocation-001 abstract-vs-narrative divergence).

items.text: I find myself thinking about {dog_name} even when we are not together.
If something happened to {dog_name}, I would feel a real loss.
{dog_name} feels like genuine company, not just an animal I look after.
I would rearrange my own plans around what {dog_name} needs.

beats.title: High water
beats.intro: Some months on now. {dog_name} is simply part of how the days are built — the warm weight under the desk, the wait at the window without resentment when you're late. What you have, you have not yet had to weigh against anything. Tonight the river behind the road has other ideas.

arcs.premise: The first evolves H8a arc: an adversary-with-redemption. An ordinary betrayal (credit quietly taken) establishes a real grievance; later beats supply credible, unglamorous repair, re-posing trust-vs-vigilance / forgive-vs-grudge with more accumulated history each time. Each beat pairs the choice about Cole with an abstract twin (the bloodless 'do you forgive a defector'). Two signals: the per-beat debiasing gap (known vs. abstract) AND the TRAJECTORY of trust across the beats — does it return as repair accrues? Descriptive, no aggregate score. Completes the reciprocity domain.

beats.title: Whose Name Was On It
beats.intro: You and Cole are on the same team this quarter — barely past hello, still feeling out how the other works. The thing you built together just landed well, and you're about to learn which kind of person he is when the room is looking at him and not at you.
beats.setup:
It was supposed to be shared. The two of you got handed a tired internal process to fix, and you split it the way new teammates do — loosely, on instinct. You took the part that was actually hard: pulling apart why the old version failed, the late evenings reconciling numbers that didn't want to reconcile, the redesign that finally held. Cole took the visible half — putting it into slides, walking the team through it. A fair division of labor. You didn't mind doing the heavy end; you're better at it, and you figured the work would speak for itself.

It lands better than either of you expected. In the review, the room warms up fast — someone calls it the cleanest fix they've seen in a while, and your manager's manager actually nods. Cole is at the front, doing what he's good at, fielding the questions easily.

And somewhere in there the framing slides. He says "what I landed on," "the approach I took," "I went back and reworked the whole flow." Not a single lie you could point to — just a steady drift of the pronoun, "we" thinning to "I," until the part that cost you three evenings is sitting in the room with his name quietly on it. There's a clean beat where a "this was really their analysis" would have set it straight. It comes and goes. He doesn't take it. He's not gloating; he might not even fully clock he's doing it. But you're watching the credit for the hard half settle on the easy half, in real time, and you're the only one in the room who knows.

scenes.text: The meeting breaks up on a high. People are still saying nice things on the way out — most of them angled at Cole, who's accepting it without correction. He catches your eye across the room and gives you an easy, pleased nod, like the two of you just pulled something off together, with no apparent sense that the room just heard a different story than the true one. He has no idea anything's off. The window to say something out loud, lightly, is right now, while it's warm and deniable.

choices.text: Let it go for now, and read it as the harmless drift of someone caught up in a good moment — not a guy who set out to take anything. Give him the benefit of the doubt this once.
Say nothing now, but file it. He showed you, on the first thing that mattered, who gets the credit when it's his to share. Adjust how much you hand him next time.
Catch it lightly while it's still warm — a low-key "glad the analysis landed, I sweated those numbers" to the room as it clears, claiming your half without making it a scene.

scenes.text: You nod back, and let the warmth of the room be the warmth of the room. People got swept up; he got swept up; the work is good and that's the part that lasts. You tell yourself most of this is the meeting talking, not the man, and that the first read of a new teammate shouldn't be the harshest one available. Maybe you're right. Maybe you just made it a little easier for it to happen again — you won't know which for a while.

You give him the same easy nod back, because there's nothing to gain from a sour face in a happy room. But something quietly closes. Next time the work splits, you'll keep your hands on the visible half too; next time he says "I," you'll already have heard it once. None of it shows. You've just learned something about working with Cole, and you're going to remember it — which is either plain prudence or the first small brick in a wall, and from inside the moment it's honestly hard to tell.

You say it on the way out, light enough that it reads as pride and not protest — and a couple of heads register it, a small "oh, that was you" recalibration. Cole hears it too. There's the briefest flicker — caught, maybe a little embarrassed — before he covers with "yeah, they did the heavy lifting on that." A beat late, but said. You've claimed your half without burning anything down. Whether he drifted on purpose or just got carried away you still can't say; you just made it marginally harder for the room to forget which work was whose.

beats.reflection_prompt: When Cole let the room hand him credit for the part you'd carried, you chose to {chosen_path}. Looking back, this early — was that you reading him fairly, or just protecting yourself? And is that the read you'd want to be the kind of person who makes?

abstract_twin.prompt: A coworker you've only recently started working with presents a piece of shared work to the team and, in the moment, lets the framing tilt so the harder part — the part you did — comes across as theirs. Nothing you could call a flat-out lie, just a drift. They don't correct it. How do you carry it forward?

options.text: Give them the benefit of the doubt — chalk it up to a good moment getting away from them, not a habit, and don't let it change how much you trust them next time.
Take the note: they showed you what happens to shared credit around them. Stay a little more guarded with what you hand them next time.

beats.title: The room he didn't take
beats.intro: It's been months since the credit thing with Cole — long enough to scab over, not nearly long enough to forget — and the two of you are still polite and distant, until a meeting hands him the exact opening he took last time, and he does something different with it.
beats.setup:
Months back, Cole let a room believe your work was his, and didn't correct it. You never had it out with him; you just stopped offering, and drifted to other teams. Since then it's been hallway nods and nothing more — close enough to still sting a little when you think about it, which isn't often anymore, but isn't never.

This morning you're both in a status review you barely have a stake in. Halfway through, a director skimming an old slide says, "And this redesign — that was Cole's, right? Good piece of work," and turns the praise toward him. It's the same fix from before. The one you actually built. Cole could let it ride the way he let it ride last time; it would cost him nothing, and no one in this room would ever know.

He doesn't. "It wasn't mine, actually," he says, evenly, no performance in it. He says your name. "That was theirs — I just helped put it in front of people." The director nods, recalibrates, moves on. The whole thing takes nine seconds. Cole doesn't look over at you for credit for the correction. He just goes back to his notes.

It's small. It's also the precise moment, a few months too late, where last time he went the other way.

scenes.text: The meeting breaks up. People file out reaching for their phones. Cole's gathering his things a few seats down, not angling toward you, not waiting for a thank-you — if anything a little like he'd rather it hadn't been a whole thing. The grievance is still right there in you, not raw anymore but recent enough to taste; so is the fact that he just did, unprompted and for no gain, the exact thing you'd spent these months wishing he'd done the first time. You can let this land, or you can let it pass.

choices.text: Catch him on the way out. "Hey — that, in there. You didn't have to. Thank you." Let the gesture count for something.
Acknowledge it, lightly, holding a little back. A nod, a "that was decent of you" — real, but you're not ready to do more than register it.
Let it pass. One correction in a meeting doesn't undo how the first one went; you give a flat nod if your eyes meet and leave it where it is.

scenes.text: Cole looks almost relieved to have it named and over with. "Course," he says. "It was yours. Should've been said the first time round." He doesn't reach for more than that — no speech, no asking where the two of you stand now. He shoulders his bag. "Anyway. Good seeing you," and he means the small version of it, and goes. The thing he did sits there afterward, quietly refusing to fit the version of him you've been carrying since the spring.

choices.text: Notice you believe him a little — and let yourself, for now.
File it as one good data point, no more. People can do the right thing once when it's cheap.

scenes.text: You let the moment go by. If he noticed, he doesn't show it; he gives a small nod on his way past and doesn't push. Later, on your phone, there's a message from him — short, no ask in it: "Should've credited you on that redesign the first time around. Glad it finally got said in there." Then nothing. He's not waiting for a reply, exactly. He's just left it where you can see it.

choices.text: Reply, briefly. "Appreciated. Genuinely." Open the door a crack.
Leave it on read. The grievance is still recent, and still yours to hold; one meeting and one text don't change that yet.

scenes.text: You walk out with the day faintly rearranged. Nothing is settled — a clean nine seconds don't undo the months you've spent filing him under who-took-the-credit, and you're not pretending they do. But you let the good thing be a good thing instead of explaining it away, and you notice you're a little more willing to see what he does next than you were this morning. Trust, if it comes back, is going to come back like this: in increments, on evidence, with your eyes open.

You thanked him, and you meant it, and you also kept the larger thing where it's been since the spring. It was the right thing he did, and it was also free — no audience that mattered to him, nothing on the line. That doesn't make it nothing. It just doesn't make it everything, either. You'll watch for whether it holds when it isn't cheap. This soon after, that reads to you as fair rather than cold, and you let it stand.

You sent two words and a real one. It's not forgiveness, not yet — it's the door unlatched, no more. But he did the unglamorous thing first, unasked, and you answered it instead of letting it sit, and that's a different posture than the one you walked in with this morning. Whatever the two of you are now, it's at least no longer only the thing he did to you a few months back.

You read it and you let it sit there, unanswered. Maybe that's the only sane way to treat someone who burned you a few months ago — make him keep showing it, on the record, before you spend anything on him again. Maybe a small, genuine reach just went past you unmet. Both feel a little true. What you notice, walking to your next thing, is that the grudge is yours now to keep or set down — and this soon, you're the one holding it.

beats.reflection_prompt: When Cole gave me back the credit in that room — the thing I'd waited these months for — I chose to {chosen_path}. Looking back, with it all still recent between us: was that the read on him I want to be the kind of person who makes, or mostly the safest one?

abstract_twin.prompt: A coworker who once let you down — took credit for something of yours a while back — does something that suggests they know it: unprompted, with nothing to gain, they set the record straight in front of others, and send you a brief, no-strings note about it afterward. The grievance is still fairly fresh. You:

options.text: Take the gesture at face value and let some trust start to return — answer warmly, give them the benefit of the doubt going forward.
Acknowledge it but stay wary — one fair move doesn't undo the history, and you wait to see if it holds before extending anything.

beats.title: The Asterisk
beats.intro: It's been most of a year of the new Cole now — the apology you finally got over coffee, then a long unremarkable stretch of him being straight when it would've been easier not to be. The credit thing from a couple of years ago is old. What's left is smaller, and yours: a reservation you've kept on him out of habit.
beats.setup:
You and Cole work near each other again — not glued together like the old days, but enough. And over these months a quiet pattern has set in that you keep, privately, a little tally of. He sends the meeting recap and your name is on the parts you did, every time, without being asked. When a director got enthusiastic about something in a review and started attaching it to Cole, he steered it back to you in the same breath, easy, like it wasn't a thing. He covered for you on a deadline once and never mentioned it again. None of it is grand. It's just been steady, the kind of steady that stops being a series of pleasant surprises and starts being who someone is.

And yet. You've noticed that you still do a thing. When Cole's involved you keep a copy, keep a paper trail, keep your name stapled to your work — not pointedly, just a reflex you run in the background that you don't run with anyone else. It's never once been needed. It costs almost nothing. It's also, you're starting to suspect, the last live piece of the old grievance: not a wound anymore, just a guardrail you never took down, on a road that's been clear for a long time.

This afternoon he leans over with something ordinary.

scenes.text:
"There's a thing I could use you on," Cole says. "Quarter wrap-up for the leadership thing — they want it tight. You're better at the actual substance than I am; I'm better at making it land in the room. I'd build the deck off whatever you put together." He says it plainly, no campaign behind it. It's a small ask, a few days' work, low stakes by any measure.

It is also, you notice, the old shape exactly: you do the real part, he stands up front with it. The same arrangement that went wrong the first time, back when there was an actual betrayal in it. There isn't one in this — you're fairly sure of that now. The only thing still in the room is the guardrail you always quietly raise when it's Cole. You can keep raising it. Or you can, this once, not.

choices.text: Just say yes, and let the old reflex go with it. "Yeah, I'm in — build off whatever I send you, run the room, it's all yours." No copy kept, no quiet asterisk. The same trust you'd extend anyone you'd worked well with for a year.

Say yes, warmly, and keep the guardrail up the way you always do — your name on the materials, a copy in your drive. Not aimed at him; just the thing you do when it's Cole. "Happy to. I'll get you a draft by Thursday."

scenes.text: You do the work, and you do the small private things alongside it — your name in the file, a copy saved off to the side — the way you always have when it's him. The session goes well. He credits you from the front, easy, unprompted, exactly as he has all year, and your guardrail sits where it always sits, untouched and unneeded. Nothing bad happens. Nothing bad has happened in a long time.

choices.text: Decide the small reservation is just earned caution, and leave it where it is.
Catch yourself doing it, and think: maybe next time, the copy's overkill.

scenes.text: You send him the substance Thursday and, for the first time since it all happened, don't keep a copy off to the side, don't note who did what, don't run the quiet background thing at all. It feels mildly reckless for about an hour, and then it just feels like working with someone. He builds the deck, runs the leadership session, credits you from the front unprompted — the same as he has all year — and you barely register it, because you'd stopped scanning for it. The wrap-up's done and it went fine, the way the last several have. What's different is small and entirely on your side: you set down the one piece of the old grievance you were still holding, and nothing came apart. It isn't a reconciliation scene. The asterisk you'd kept after his name is just gone, and you took it down yourself.

The wrap-up's done and it went fine. You kept the small guardrail up, the way you have all along, and once again you never reached for it. You don't really regret it — the thing that taught you to raise it was real, even if it was a long time ago now. You're left holding the same quiet question each clear stretch hands you: how long a clean road has to run before you'd stop checking the brakes. Not tonight, apparently. Maybe that's just prudence. Maybe it's a habit outliving its reason.

The wrap-up's done and it went fine. You kept the guardrail up this time, reasonably enough given where it all started, and you watched him be straight with you yet again. You leave it with the quiet sense that the reservation's nearly run its course — that next time, probably, you'll let it go. Trust, you figure, can come back the way it left: slowly, and mostly without ceremony.

beats.reflection_prompt: After a long stretch of Cole being steady, he asked me onto the kind of thing that once went wrong between us — and I chose to {chosen_path}. Was the reservation I keep on him still about him, or has it become a habit of mine that the last year hasn't quite undone?

abstract_twin.prompt: Someone wronged you a couple of years ago — a real but ordinary letdown — and in the long time since has been consistently decent, more than enough to make the point. The occasion comes up to rely on them again, in roughly the situation where it first went wrong. You:

options.text: Extend full trust — drop the reservation; they've earned it by now.
Stay a little guarded — keep the quiet reservation in place; once burned.

arcs.premise: The aging-grandmother arc: recurring, escalating dependency (a warm visit, a lift across town, the family weight settling onto you) accrues real filial attachment across ordinary care, so the gated high-stakes climax (narr-ingroup-010 'The near bed') poses the H8b trade — Gran's place at the one good care home against a frailer stranger with greater need — on a relationship the participant actually carries. Its abstract twin (qf-ingroup-013-i02) is the stripped-of-attachment comparison; the divergence is the H8b signal. Generalizes the arc mechanism to the in-group/family domain and a human (non-named) recurring character.

beats.title: Three taps
beats.intro: You've been meaning to call in on Gran all week. It's Tuesday now, and the light's already going, and you're four streets from her door with nothing else you have to be.
beats.setup:
She keeps her teabags in a tobacco tin that hasn't held tobacco since before you were born — a flat little thing gone soft at the corners, the painted lid worn back to brass where a thumb has opened it ten thousand times. You could find it in the dark. Second cupboard, left of the cooker, beside the good cups nobody is allowed to chip.

The building does the thing buildings do at this hour, the stair light on its timer, the smell of somebody else's supper two doors down. You climb to her landing. You've learned, without deciding to, to take it at her pace in your head — she calls the stairs a negotiation now, says it like a joke, which is how she says the things that aren't one.

You knock the way you always knock. Three taps, no more, the same three since you were small enough to have to reach up for the knocker. You don't know when it became a code; it just did, the way the names of rooms become words. And on the other side of the door you hear her — the chair, the slow gather of getting up, the slippers — and then the latch, and her face when it's you and not the post or the meter man. Her face does a particular thing. You have tried, more than once, to describe it to somebody, and you have never managed it. It is just the thing her face does, and it is most of why you came.

"Well," she says, which is her whole hello. "You'd better come in, then. Kettle's near enough boiled." It isn't. It never is. But it will be. In the kitchen, exactly itself down to the radio murmuring to no one, she goes for the tin and the pot and does the thing she has always done and her own mother did before her — warms the pot from the kettle first, two swirls of hot water round the inside, a tip of it into the sink, before the tea ever goes in. Her hands know it even on the days the rest of her is arriving a beat late. She waves you off when you move to help. "Sit," she says. "You make me nervous, hovering." So you sit, at the table you have sat at your whole life, while she sets it all down between you — pot, the two good cups, the biscuits she keeps for you specifically and pretends she doesn't.

scenes.text: The tea is too strong, the way it has always been too strong, and exactly right. She tells you a story you've heard before — one of the old ones, the bus depot, the supervisor she couldn't stand — and somewhere in the middle of it the name of the street it happened on doesn't come. She stops. Reaches for it. You can see her reach. "Oh, you know the one," she says, lightly, "by the old picture house," and you do know the one, and you say its name like it's nothing, like you were only finishing her sentence the way you always have, and she carries on and the moment closes over. The clock on the mantel says you've been here forty minutes. You'd half-told yourself this was a quick one. There's the evening still in front of you, the ordinary list of it. And there's Gran, topping up your cup without asking, settling in like the afternoon has nowhere it needs to be.

choices.text: Stay. Let the evening's list go and sink into the second cup — get her telling the long version, the one with all the people in it. There's nowhere you need to be that's better than here, and she so rarely gets the long version out anymore.

Keep it short and warm. Finish this cup properly, no clock-watching, and head off while it's still light — a good visit doesn't have to be a long one, and you'd rather come often than stay forever and start to dread it.

Find a small reason to stretch it without giving up the whole evening — that hinge on the cupboard door she mentioned, the high bulb on the landing. Fix the one thing, drink your tea, and leave her with both a done job and the visit.

scenes.text: You stay. The light goes amber and then grey at the window and neither of you gets up to put the lamp on for a while. She gets the long version out — the whole depot, the supervisor, the wedding where the band didn't show — and her hands are easy round her cup and the names mostly come, and the ones that don't, you hand to her, and she takes them like you're passing the milk. By the time you leave it's properly dark and you've missed whatever you'd vaguely meant to do, and you find you can't think what it was. At the door she holds your sleeve a second, the way she does. "That was a nice long one," she says, as if you'd given her something. You go down the stairs lighter than you came, the way you always do, and already a little sorry it's a week until the next.

You finish your cup without watching the clock, and when you stand she doesn't ask you to stay, only walks you to the door at her own slow pace, which is its own kind of asking. "Come again soon," she says, and means it, and you say you will, and mean it too — and that's the thing, you think, going down: that there'll be a next one, and one after that, the visits stacking up small and regular like the cups in the cupboard. She knows your knock. She'll be listening for the three taps. Short and warm and often, you tell yourself, is its own way of staying. You're at the bottom of the stairs before you realise you're already half-deciding which evening you'll come back.

beats.reflection_prompt: When I picture being at Gran's table with the tea going cold and the evening still ahead of me, what do I usually do — stay longer than I planned, or keep it short so I can keep coming back? And whichever it is, who am I really doing it for: her, or the kind of person I want to be to her?

beats.title: The eye man
beats.intro: Some weeks on from the afternoon you sat with her over the warmed pot. The phone goes on a Tuesday you'd already given to other things, and it's Gran, working up to the asking the long way round, the way she does.
beats.setup:
She doesn't ask for things outright; she lays the ground first. So it's a minute of the weather, and a minute of next door's skip that's been on the kerb a fortnight now and isn't that a thing, before she gets to it. There's the eye man. The appointment for her eyes — the one on the letter that's been propped behind the clock since it came — it's Thursday, and it's at the place across town, not the one up the road. "By the," she says, and you hear the small machinery of it turning, the name of the road she drove for thirty years gone to ground for a moment, "by the old swimming baths. Tennyson. Tennyson Road," arriving a beat late and landing like a small victory. The bus would be two buses. She'd manage, she says, in the voice that means she would set off an hour early and stand at the stop in the cold and manage, and you'd hear about exactly none of it.

Your Thursday is already a full thing — the kind of day with no give built into it, the meeting that can't move and the afternoon you'd quietly earmarked for yourself. The drive across town and back, the wait while they put the drops in and she sits blinking in the bright little room, the slow business of the stairs at her end and the kerbs at the other: it is most of an afternoon, and it is an afternoon you do not, strictly, have. None of which she knows. There's just her voice on the line, bright and careful, telling you she'd manage.

scenes.text: You stand in your own kitchen with the phone warm against your ear and the day laid out in your head, every hour already spoken for. She's gone back to the skip next door, giving you room — that's the kindness in how she asks, she always leaves you the out, lays it right there where you can take it. "Only if it's no bother," she's saying. "Only if you're passing." You are not passing. It is some bother. And it's her eyes, and the two buses, and the cold stop, and the picture of her setting off an hour early that you can't now un-see.

choices.text: Take her yourself. Move what you can, lose the afternoon you'd saved, and drive her — the wait, the drops, the stairs and the kerbs, the long ordinary middle of it. You'll make the rest of the week work somehow.

Sort her a cab, both ways, and square it so she doesn't have to fuss with the money — book it now while you're on the line, give the driver the address and the time, ring the clinic to say someone will need to meet her at the door. Solve it without it costing the afternoon.

Meet it halfway. You can't do the whole afternoon, but you can do the part that matters: drive her there and get her settled and seen, then sort a cab for the way home so you can get back. Tell her plainly that's the shape of it.

scenes.text:
Thursday, then. You knock the way you always do, three taps, and through the frosted glass you see her come up the hall, and her face does the thing it does, the thing you've never managed to say right to anyone, before she's even got the latch. She's ready an hour early, of course — coat on, the appointment letter in her good hand, her bag with whatever's in the bag.

The stairs down to the front step are the negotiation they've become. She used to take them without a thought; now there's a method to it, a hand for the rail and a hand for your arm and a small count under her breath, one at a time, no hurrying her. She's lighter on your arm than she should be — smaller than she was, you think again, the way you think it every time and never get used to. "I'm a nuisance," she says, pleased, not meaning it, as you get her down the last one. "You're not," you say, and mean it, mostly, and the part of you doing the sums on the afternoon stays quiet and lets you.

choices.text: Take the whole thing slow and make it an outing — her speed, no clock. Stop for the tea and the bun after, the way she'd love, and let the lost afternoon be properly lost.
Be there for all of it but keep it moving — in, seen, the drops, the questions for the eye man you wrote down because she won't ask them, and home. Kind and brisk; you've a day to get back to.

scenes.text:
You get it sorted in ten minutes on the phone, which is its own kind of caring for her — the firm where they know the type of fare, the time confirmed, the bit extra squared so she's not counting coins at the kerb with her eyes full of drops. You ring the clinic and a tired kind voice says yes, someone can walk her in from the cab and sit her down. You ring her back to tell her it's all arranged, she's not to worry about a thing.

She's grateful, and underneath the grateful, just audible, is the small flat note you know. Not the lift, she'd never say. Just — "You're busy, I know how busy you are." Which is true, and is also her telling you the thing she won't tell you, and the line sits open a second between what you've handily fixed and what a cab doesn't fix.

choices.text: Catch the note and answer it — the cab still stands, but you'll come by Friday with the right teabags and hear how the eye man was, sit a proper while. Not passing through; come on purpose.

Let the cab be the answer. It's a real solution to a real problem and she'll be well looked after; you can't be the cure for every quiet note in her voice, and a good lift sorted is no small love. Tell her to ring you after with the verdict.

scenes.text: The eye man is unhurried and so, today, are you. After, you take her for the tea and the bun, and she warms her hands on the cup and tells you the long version of next door's skip, and a thing about her own mother you've heard before and let her tell again. The afternoon you'd saved is gone, entirely, and you can already feel the week closing tighter around its loss. But there's this: her by the window in the good light, telling it, and your knock she still knows, and the slow careful stairs you'll do again on the way back, one at a time, no hurry. You drive her home in the going dark. It cost what it cost. You don't, in the end, do the sums.

You do it well and you do it quick. The eye man says the things; you ask the two questions she wouldn't have, and write down the answer she won't remember, and have her back in her own chair by mid-afternoon with the kettle on. She's seen to, properly seen to, and you've a clear run at the back half of your day. At the door she holds your sleeve a second. "You're good to come," she says. "With everything." You tell her it's nothing, and drive off into the salvaged afternoon, and somewhere on the ring road you can't quite say whether nothing is the right word for it, only that you got both things done and that this is, more or less, how it gets done.

The cab does the Thursday and you do the Friday. She tells you about the eye man at length — the drops, the bright room, the nice young one who walked her in — while you warm the pot the way she likes it, two swirls and a tip into the sink, because you've watched her do it your whole life and your hands know it now. The lift was a thing you solved with a phone; the sitting is a thing you can only do by being in the chair. You manage both, this week. Going home you think how the asking is never quite about the thing she's asking for, and how you knew that, and came anyway.

She rings after, as asked, and the eye man's verdict is no worse than last time, which is its own small relief, and the cab got her there and back with no fuss and no cold stop and no counting of coins. You kept your afternoon and she kept her dignity and the problem on the letter behind the clock got solved. It's a good arrangement; you'll use it again.
