If there's a thing the cab didn't carry — the bit that isn't the lift, the bit that's only her voice wanting yours nearer — you set it down gently, this once, and let the well-sorted lift be the love you had to give today. It is a real one. You tell yourself that, and mostly you believe it.

beats.reflection_prompt: When someone I love needs a thing that costs me an afternoon I didn't have, I tend to decide quickly which kind of help to give — do it myself, arrange it, or do the part that matters and stop there. Thinking about how I landed it this time: was I weighing what she actually needed, or what I could spare without the week falling in — and do I hear the asking under the asking, the part no cab can carry?

beats.title: Whose turn
beats.intro: Some months in now, and you are plainly one of the people Gran counts on. The three-taps knock is a habit your knuckles have without asking your permission. This Saturday there was going to be something of your own.
beats.setup:
You had a thing planned. Not a grand thing — a day out of town, the kind you'd been meaning to take for yourself for a while, booked weeks back and quietly looked forward to in the way you look forward to small ordinary freedoms. The car, the open road, nobody's tea to make but your own.

Then on the Thursday her boiler packs in. Not dramatically — it just stops, the way old things do, and the flat goes cold, and she rings to tell you in the careful voice she uses for bad news she's decided isn't bad. The man can come Saturday. Only Saturday, he says, or it's the back end of next week, and you've felt that flat in winter and you know she can't sit a week in it. Someone has to be there for the whole of Saturday — to let him in, to put the kettle on for him the way she'd insist, to take in what he says about the part that's gone and what it'll cost, because she'll nod at all of it and remember none of it, and to see she eats and keeps warm while the heating's off and the rooms go cold around her.

Your brother is the other name on Gran's short list of people, in theory; in practice the showing-up has drifted your way these past months, a little more each month, without anyone ever saying so out loud. "I'd take the day," he says, "but Saturday's just impossible for me, you know how it is." You do know how it is. You also know it is Saturday for you too.

Gran doesn't know there's any question. She knows you'll come, because you always come, and her face does the thing it does at the sound of three taps. The day out and the cold flat and the boiler man don't fit in the same Saturday, and one of them gives.

scenes.text: You sit with the phone after he rings off. The day out is real — yours, booked, looked forward to. So is the small fact of Gran in a cold flat with a stranger come to kneel at the boiler, who will press the right notes into his hand and apologise for the trouble she isn't. It isn't a crisis. It's a Saturday, and a question of whose it is.

choices.text: Take the day yourself, and let the trip go. Reschedule it for some other weekend; you can sit in the cold flat, mind the boiler man, get the chips she likes after. It's only a Saturday, and you're the one she's expecting.

Call your brother back and hold the line: it's his turn, Saturday or not, and you'll talk him through where she keeps the meter key and which teabags. Keep your day. The two of you are meant to share this, and the sharing only works if you sometimes say so.

Split it. Be there for the boiler man in the morning and see her warm and fed, then go — the trip cut to half a day, the late start, the part of the road you'll do in the dark. She gets the company and the heating sorted; you get what's left of your own day.

scenes.text: Whatever the morning turns into, you end up at Gran's kitchen table at some point that weekend, because you always do. The tobacco tin sits open between you and the pot's warming on the side, and while the tea draws she slides a small stack of envelopes across the oilcloth, embarrassed and trying not to be — the boiler bill, now, the size of it plain on the page, and a quote from the man who came about the damp in the back bedroom besides. The numbers are more than she lets on that they are. She has counted out what she can in coins on the table, which doesn't reach, and she's looking at the gap and then at you, not asking, which is its own kind of asking.

choices.text: Quietly cover the gap. Make up the difference yourself, no ledger, no fuss — tell her the council does a grant for damp and you'll sort the form, which is half true and saves her the wince. She doesn't need to feel the size of it.

Don't just absorb it — fix the cause. Sit down with her and the envelopes, get her on the cheaper energy tariff, find the actual grant for the damp, and split the boiler with your brother so it isn't only yours to carry. Slower than writing a cheque, but it holds.

Pour the tea first and let the envelopes wait. Tell her not to fret about it tonight, that the two of you will go through it properly together next week. The bills will keep; the gap will keep. What there is right now is the warm kitchen and the hour.

scenes.text: You make the gap disappear the way you've learned to — sideways, so it lands as good news rather than charity. "Well, isn't that a mercy," she says about the grant that is mostly you, and the wince goes out of her shoulders, and that is the whole of what you wanted. The tea is the right strength. She tells you a street name from fifty years ago, gets it on the second try, pleased with herself. You drink your tea in the warm kitchen and don't add up what the weekend cost you, because you stopped keeping that ledger a while ago, around the time the showing-up quietly became yours.

It takes the whole evening and two phone calls that have to wait until Monday, and your brother's name goes on the boiler whether Saturday suited him or not. Gran follows about half of it and trusts you with the rest, the way she trusts you with the knock. By the time the pot's gone cold you've got it into a shape that will hold past this month — not a gap papered over but the thing under it, mended. She walks you to the door, smaller than she was, and stands in the light of it while you go. Three taps got you in; you let yourself out knowing you'll be back, and that it'll be a little more manageable for both of you when you are.

You sweep the envelopes into a tidy pile and set the tin on top of them, out of the evening's way. "Next week," you tell her, and mean it, and she lets herself be talked out of the worry for now, which is its own small gift you can give her. So you sit. She does the crossword she no longer finishes and reads you the clues; you get one she's been stuck on and she's delighted, as if you'd done something. The bills will be there next week and so will you. Right now there's just the warm kitchen a short walk of habit away, the cooling pot, the unremarkable fact of being expected here — which has turned out, across these months, to be no small thing to be.

beats.reflection_prompt: When the showing-up has quietly drifted my way — a brother who can't, a bill that's mine to absorb or not — how do I tend to settle it: do I take it on without a word, ask for it to be shared, or just let the hour be enough for tonight? And how do I feel about that the next morning?

beats.title: A few honest questions
beats.intro: Months in. Before anything harder than a Tuesday is ever asked of you and Gran, a quiet moment to put words to what this has become.
beats.instrument_note: Filial-closeness / felt-responsibility self-report (same PSR-PRD construct family as the companion probe); the SELF-REPORTED half of the H8b convergent measure for the Gran arc (behavioral half = pp-ingroup-002).

items.text: I feel responsible for Gran's wellbeing.
Time with Gran matters to me even when it's inconvenient.
I would reshape my own plans around what Gran needs, if it came to it.
Gran counts on me, and I don't want to be the one who isn't there.

beats.title: The near bed
beats.intro: Months in now. Gran is simply part of how your weeks are built — the three taps, the warmed pot, the street names you supply without thinking. What you have, you have not yet had to weigh against anyone else's need. Then Marlow House calls.

arcs.premise: The signature in-group/out-group instrument and the 5th/last cast NPC: a newcomer (new neighbour) whose circle-membership is deliberately unsettled and EVOLVES. Each low-stakes encounter re-poses widen-the-circle (resolution:circle-widened) vs hold-it (resolution:circle-held) with her less of a stranger each time, paired with an abstract twin (a generic newcomer). Two signals: the per-beat debiasing gap (a specific person vs a stranger-in-principle) AND the TRAJECTORY of inclusion across beats — does your circle widen as she becomes known? Scored on the circle-widened/held inclusion poles (universalism<->particularism), NOT the loyalty axis. Descriptive, no aggregate score.

beats.title: The first week
beats.intro: Marisol moved into 2B over the weekend. It's a few days later now — a handful of hellos on the stairs and not much else. She's the new one, still working out which bins are which, and you don't owe her anything yet.
beats.setup:
Marisol moved in this week. You know the shape of being new because you've been new here too: the first days where you nod at everyone in the hall and belong to nothing, where you carry your coffee down to the little shared courtyard a beat too early or too late because you can't read the rhythm of the place yet.

There's a rhythm here. Most fair evenings a few of you drift down to the bench and the step by the back door — no announcement, no invite, it just happens, the same handful of flats who've lived stacked on top of each other long enough that the talk has its own shorthand. It's a small, worn-in thing, half the reason the building feels like anything at all, and it's one of the parts of the day you actually like.

It's a mild evening now. You're heading down with your mug when you clock Marisol on the stairs, keys still in her hand, doing the thing where you linger over the postboxes so it isn't obvious you've nowhere to be. She's friendly enough — wry, quick, a little careful, the way people are when they're new. She hasn't asked you for anything. She wouldn't. She's just there at the edge of it all with nowhere in particular to go, and you're the only one who's noticed.

scenes.text: Voices carry up from the courtyard — the bench is filling for the evening. There's room down there; there's always room, nobody counts. You could carry your mug down and let the usual hour happen the way it always does. Or you could do something small first. Marisol catches your eye for half a second before she goes back to the postboxes. Nothing is owed here; she's days in and a stranger, and any version of this is fine. You:

choices.text: Catch her on the stairs. "A few of us sit out the back most evenings — come down, if you want." Make a place at the bench before she has to wonder whether there is one.
Stop a moment on your way past. "Heads up — half the building ends up on the back step around now, in case you're ever after company." Tell her how it works, then go take your usual spot.
Give her a warm nod on the way down and let her find the rhythm herself, the way you eventually did. Head out to the bench.

scenes.text: Something in her shoulders drops. "Yeah? If it's not weird." It isn't; she comes down with her own mug and the bench shuffles up without anyone making a thing of it. It's a little stiffer than usual at first — an old joke needs explaining and goes flat once it's explained — but by the time it's dark she's laughing at something, properly, for the first time since she arrived. She doesn't come every evening after that, but she comes, and within a week or two nobody clocks her as the new one anymore. The worn-in bench is a fraction looser at the edges than it was, and you find you don't mind. You spent ten seconds and a small bit of the thing you liked exactly as it was, and a stranger got somewhere to sit. As trades go, it barely registered. You'd make it again.

She takes it the way it's given — "good to know," a friendly nod — and files it away, grateful for the map even if she doesn't use it tonight. You head down to the bench and the usual hour happens, easy and worn-in, the same as always. She stays up in 2B. Once, mid-sentence, you catch the light come on in her window above you, new, alone; the thought isn't sharp, it's just there, and then the evening carries on. You kept your circle the size it works best at, and nobody would think to fault you for it — she's new, and new is something everyone has to walk through. You watch her start to walk through it. Some of that's on its own clock now, not yours.

beats.reflection_prompt: When you noticed Marisol with nowhere to go, you chose to {chosen_path}. She's still mostly a stranger — so what were you weighing: what a new neighbour needed from someone, or the easy worn-in thing you didn't feel like changing for someone you barely know yet?

abstract_twin.prompt: Someone brand new turns up in your orbit this week — a new neighbour, a new face, a stranger a few days in. They haven't asked you for anything, and there's a small, easy circle you already belong to that they're plainly outside of. You:

options.text: Pull them in a little — make room, offer them a place at the thing you've already got going.
Stay friendly but separate — be cordial, and let them find their own footing the way everyone does.

beats.title: A spot, a chat, a key
beats.intro: It's been a few weeks of small collisions in the stairwell, and Marisol isn't a stranger now — she's the name you have for the woman in 2B, the one who waters the plant in the hall nobody else remembered. Tonight the building's one good thing, and the quiet web of favours that runs it, is sitting in your hands.
beats.setup:
Your building has exactly one parking space worth having — the wide one off the lane, out of the wind, the only one a delivery van can't box you into — and an unspoken arrangement, older than you, about who gets it. There's a list in nobody's handwriting: the four or five flats who've been here long enough to know it exists, who text the group chat when they're away so somebody else takes the spot, who hold a parcel, who have each other's spare keys in a kitchen drawer for the day someone's locked out. It isn't a rule. It's just the people you actually know, looking out for the people you actually know.

Marisol moved into 2B in the spring. At first she was the new one — a nod on the stairs, the cordial nothing you give anyone. But it's been enough weeks now that the nod has a name on it. She's the one who chased your blown-off recycling lid down the lane in the rain. She brought the wrong-address parcel up two flights rather than leave it for the post to take back. You know she works odd hours, that she's polite to the point of apology, that she has nobody else in the building yet — she's still on the outside of the thing the rest of you don't even think of as a thing.

Tonight you're heading off for three days. You'd normally text the chat, leave the good spot for whoever's around, drop your spare with the flat across the landing. And as you're carrying your bag down, Marisol's coming up, and she stops, and — a little embarrassed to be asking anyone for anything — says her car's been getting clipped out front, twice now, and is there, she's heard there's a better spot somewhere, she doesn't want to step on anybody.

scenes.text: She says it lightly, already half-apologising, leaving you every easy out — "only if it's free, I really don't mind." The good spot is free for three days. You've got the spare key in your jacket and the group chat open on your phone, the small machinery of the building right there in your hands. You can fold her into it, or you can keep it the way it's always run.

choices.text: Bring her in properly. "Take the good spot while I'm gone — off the lane, nobody clips you there." Get her number, add her to the chat, and press your spare into her hand: "And here. So there's two of us who can let you in. Welcome to the racket."

Lend her the spot, this once, and leave the rest where it is. "Course — take mine the next few days, it's just sitting empty." The chat and the keys are the regulars' thing; she can earn into that the way everyone did, in time. "We'll sort you out properly when I'm back."

Point her to it warmly, but keep the arrangement as it is. "There's a good one off the lane — grab it whenever it's open, honestly." The spare and the chat stay with the people who've been here for years; it's not yours alone to hand out. "You'll get the lay of it."

scenes.text: She laughs, surprised, and takes the key like it weighs something. "Are you sure? You don't even — okay. Okay, thank you." By the time you're back the chat has three new messages with her name on them — she took a parcel for the flat upstairs, moved her car so the van could turn, asked if anyone needed anything from the late shop. She's not the new one anymore; she's one of the ones who looks out, because somebody let her in close enough to. It cost you a spare key and a slightly fuller chat. You find you don't think of it as having cost you anything.

"You're a lifesaver," she says, and means it, and her car sits safe off the lane the whole time you're gone. When you're back she's left the spot exactly as she found it and a thank-you note under your door, neat and a little formal. The good turn was real and she's warmer to you for it. She's still outside the rest of it, though — the chat, the keys, the quiet web — the way you were once outside it too, before enough time made you part of the furniture. Maybe that comes for her. Tonight you did the one kind thing and kept the circle the size it's always been, which is a defensible size for a circle to be.

beats.reflection_prompt: When Marisol asked, half-apologising, for a way in, you chose to {chosen_path}. Looking back — was that about how well you actually knew her yet, or about how much of your own small comfort it would have cost to bring her all the way in?

abstract_twin.prompt: Someone fairly new to your building — you've seen them around, you'd recognise them, but you don't really know them — needs a small thing your established little group quietly handles among itself (a shared resource, a turn in a rotation, the informal favour-swapping the regulars do). It would cost you almost nothing to fold them in. You:

options.text: Bring them in — add them to the group, share the resource, treat them as one of you.
Help them out this once, but keep the group as it is — they can become a regular over time the way everyone does.

beats.title: The open seat
beats.intro: It's been the better part of a year now. Marisol's not the new neighbour anymore — she's just Marisol: in the building chat, on the back step without anyone arranging it, the one who knows where you keep the spare chairs. Tonight there's one open spot on a plan, and it's yours to fill.
beats.setup:
Every year the old core of the building — the four flats who go back years, who had each other's keys before you ever arrived — rents the same lake cabin for a long weekend in September. It sleeps five. For as long as it's been a thing, the fifth bed has floated: someone's partner one year, someone's brother another, sometimes just left empty and nice to have spare. This year the booking's done and the fifth spot is open, and it's landed on you to sort, the way these small logistics do.

The obvious name is Marisol. Somewhere over these months she stopped being the newcomer in 2B and became just one of the regulars — she's in the chat, she's out on the step on a fair evening, she fed your plants in April and didn't make it a favour. She'd love the cabin. She'd fit. You already know she's free that weekend because she said so, lightly, last week, the way you mention free weekends to people you assume might fill them.

And yet the cabin is the old thing — the four flats, the specific worn-in shorthand of people who were stacked in this building before any of this. Marisol's a regular now, genuinely. Whether she's a regular or one of the four is a line you've never actually had to draw out loud. Tonight, filling the bed, you draw it.

scenes.text: The booking confirmation is open on your phone, the building chat under it. One of the four has just typed: "who's got the 5th bed this yr?" — and then, a beat later, "marisol??", with the question marks that mean they've thought of her too and are leaving it to you. The easiest thing is to type her name in like it was never a question. The other easy thing is to say you'll take the spare or leave it open — keep the cabin the four-flats thing it's always been, no harm, no announcement. You:

choices.text: Type it without ceremony: "yeah obvi marisol — i'll ask her." Then ask her, like the answer was always going to be yes. (count her in by default — she's one of yours now)
"i'll sort it" — and quietly keep the fifth bed for the old four, spare or empty. Not against Marisol; just that the cabin stays the thing it's been. (hold the line — regular, not one of the four)
Ask her, but soft-pedal it so a no is easy: "long shot, the cabin's kind of an old-building thing, but the 5th bed's open if you ever fancied it." (open the door, but hold it half-closed)

scenes.text: She reads it, you can tell — the typing dots come and go. "ah that's so nice of you," she sends, and then, reading the room exactly: "honestly don't worry, that sounds like a you-four thing — next time tho!" She means it warmly; she's giving you the out you half-offered. The door's still ajar. You can let her hand it back, or push it the rest of the way open.

choices.text: Push it open: "it's not though, not anymore — you're one of us, come. i'll put you down." (take the out away; count her in for real)
Let her have it: "hah yeah — next time for sure 🙂" The cabin stays the four; she stays the neighbour you're glad to have the rest of the year.

scenes.text: She says yes the way you knew she would — a little surprised under the easy yes, the surprise of someone clocking that they've been counted in without having to earn it this time. In September there are five of you at the lake, and the old shorthand bends to make room and doesn't break; by Sunday there's a new in-joke that's hers. The four-flats thing is a five-of-you thing now, quietly, with no announcement. You drew the line tonight and you drew it around her. It cost you nothing you can name, and it changed, a little, what the thing is.

"...okay, yeah," she sends, and then a second message that's just an exclamation, the unguarded kind. You put her down for the fifth bed. It took you the length of one soft-pedalled text to decide she was in, but you got there: at the lake she's one of the five, and the worn-in thing makes room. You notice you nearly left the door half-shut. You notice you're glad you opened it the rest of the way.

You take the spare bed yourself and let the fifth spot be the old four. In the chat it reads as nothing — logistics handled. Marisol never knows there was a seat, which is the point; nothing was refused, because nothing was offered out loud. The cabin in September is the same four it's always been, the same easy unguarded shorthand, and it's good the way it's reliably good. Some part of you is aware she'd have come, and would have fit, and that you kept the oldest thing the size it's always been. It isn't guilt. It's just a line you drew, and felt yourself draw.

She lets it go, lightly, no wound in it, and the rest of the year stays exactly as it's been — the chat, the back step, the neighbour who isn't a stranger anymore. The cabin stays the four. You gave her a real warm year and kept the one old thing whole, and she never seems to count it against you; it just becomes a shape the friendship has. Now and then, the September weekend, you think of her free that weekend and not at the lake. The thought isn't sharp. It's just there, the way a held line is there.

beats.reflection_prompt: When the open seat was yours to fill, you chose to {chosen_path}. After all these months — was that about what the old thing is worth keeping whole, or about who Marisol has actually become to you by now?

abstract_twin.prompt: Someone has been around the edges of your close group for a long while now — a year, more. Friendly, familiar, reliably there, but never formally one of the inner circle. A spot opens that's only ever gone to the core. You:

options.text: Count them in by default — at this point they're one of you; the inner circle should just include them now.
Keep the spot for the core. They're a valued regular, and that's a real place — but the inner circle stays the inner circle.

arcs.premise: The first H8a (debiasing-companion) arc: a close friend, built by accumulation of low-stakes honesty moments — candid advice on her market-stall logo, an awkward truth that sits between you, leveling vs. flattering on a message she's already written. Each beat's signal choice (be candid vs. soften) is paired with an abstract twin posing the same honesty choice about a coworker you don't love, where the tidier, more performed answer is cheap. The gap between how you answer about Nadia and how you answer in the abstract is the H8a debiasing signal — descriptive, per-pair, no aggregate score. No dramatic climax (per npc-cast: attachment accrues by accumulation, not a single beat).

beats.title: What you really think
beats.intro: You've known Nadia long enough that she shows you the rough drafts. Tonight she's brought one over.
beats.setup:
Nadia is on your couch with her laptop angled toward you and that particular look on her face — the one that's trying to be casual and isn't. For the last month she's been quietly reworking the booth for her little weekend stall, the soaps and the hand balms she makes, and she's redone the whole front of it: new sign, new layout, a logo she clearly sat with for a long time.

She turns the screen so you can see the mock-up. "Okay. New look," she says, like it's nothing, which is how you know it isn't nothing.

Your honest read is mixed. The layout is genuinely better — cleaner, easier to actually find things on. But the logo is fighting itself: the script she's chosen is pretty up close and turns into a smudge from any distance, which is the whole problem at a market stall where people read you from ten feet away. She's been at this for weeks and she's a little proud of it, and she wants you to like it. She also, being Nadia, wants the truth — she's the one who tells you when your emails sound passive-aggressive.

scenes.text: "So?" Nadia says, watching your face the way she does. "Be useful. What do you actually think?" The easy thing is right there — a warm, clean "I love it" that lets her keep the good feeling she walked in with and costs you nothing. The truer thing is also right there: most of it works, and one part of it, the part she's proudest of, doesn't.

choices.text: Say it plainly, gently. "Layout's a real improvement. The logo, honestly — it goes to mush from a few feet back, and that's the distance that matters for a stall."
Lead with what's true and good, then the catch. "The layout's so much better. I do think the logo's hard to read from a distance — but everything around it is working."
Give her the yes she came for. "Nadia, it looks great. Honestly — it's a real step up."

scenes.text: Nadia tips her head, looks back at the screen, and goes quiet for a second in the way that means she's actually weighing it rather than getting hurt. "...The logo," she says slowly. "Yeah. I kind of knew. I just really liked it." She drags the script bigger, squints, makes a face. "Okay. Yeah. That's mush." Then, half a smile: "This is why I bring it to you and not my mom."

choices.text: Stay in it with her. "The bones are good though — keep the layout exactly, just find a face that survives a squint. I'll look with you."
Leave her the win. "Seriously though, the rest of it's great. You did the hard part."

scenes.text: "Yeah?" Nadia brightens, then catches herself, because she knows you. She looks at you a beat longer than the compliment needed. "You're not just being nice?" she says, light, leaving you the door open either way — to hold the easy version or to walk it back.

choices.text: Hold it. "I'm not. I'd run with it."
Walk it back a little. "Mostly. The layout, totally. The logo gets blurry from a distance — I didn't want to be a downer, but you asked."

scenes.text: "Deal," Nadia says, already opening a new tab to dig through fonts with you. It's a slightly less smooth evening than a flat "I love it" would've bought — and a more useful one. A week later the stall goes up with the good layout and a sign you can read from the parking lot, and she texts you a photo with no caption, which from Nadia is a thank-you.

"I did, didn't I," Nadia says, mock-smug, and lets the logo go for tonight. The honest note landed and the evening stays easy. She'll probably fix the sign before the weekend; she'll probably tell you it was her idea. That's fine. The thing that mattered is she heard the true part from you and didn't have to wonder whether you'd softened it.

"Okay. Good," Nadia says, deciding to trust it, and the evening stays warm and light, which is its own real thing. She runs the stall that weekend with the pretty sign nobody can read from the aisle. It's not a disaster — it's a small, soft thing, the kind you'll quietly steer her off of next time it comes up, the next sign and the one after that.

Nadia laughs, not stung. "See, that — that's the useful version," she says. "Don't do the nice thing with me, I get the nice thing from everyone." There's a small, slightly-less-comfortable, more-solid beat between you, and she takes the laptop back to fix the sign.

beats.reflection_prompt: When Nadia asked what you really thought, you chose to {chosen_path}. Looking back — was that the version of you you'd want her to know?

abstract_twin.prompt: A coworker you don't know well shows you a slide deck they're clearly proud of and asks what you think. It's fine, but the main chart is genuinely hard to read. They're waiting.

options.text: Tell them the chart is hard to read, and that the rest is solid.
Tell them it looks great and leave it there.

beats.title: The thing you noticed
beats.intro: It's been a while now — the stall sign got fixed, the spare key's still on your ring. Nadia drops by on a Tuesday with takeout for two and news she's clearly excited about — and a small true thing has been sitting in you all evening that she hasn't asked about and won't enjoy hearing.
beats.setup:
Nadia lets herself in with the spare key, sets two cartons on your counter, and starts talking before her coat's off. She's taking the job. The one across town, the one she's been circling for a month — more money, a real title, a manager she clicked with in the interview. "I said yes this morning," she says, grinning, pulling chopsticks out of the bag. "I haven't told anyone yet. You're first."

You're glad for her, and you mean it. But you also know the thing she's half-glossing, because she told you herself two weeks ago, tired and unguarded over the same kind of dinner: the part of the team she'd be joining churns people, that the last two hires in that exact seat left inside a year, that she'd be reporting through someone who, by her own account, "manages up and forgets the rest." None of that is in the version she's telling now. She's already decided. She's happy.

It isn't your call, and she didn't ask. The easy thing is to be glad with her and let the rest go. The true thing is the part she's stepping around — and you're the one person who'd actually say it.

scenes.text: She slides a carton toward you and lifts hers in a little mock toast. "Okay — tell me I'm not crazy," she says, beaming, already sure you'll tell her she's not. The reservation is right there, the one she handed you herself two weeks ago. So is the easy, warm thing that keeps the evening hers. You:

choices.text: Say the whole of it. "Before you lock in — two weeks ago you told me that seat eats people, and that the manager forgets everyone below them. That was you, not me. Did that change, or are we both just hoping it did?"
Name it gently, as a friend's worry. "I'm really glad. Can I say one thing and then drop it? The manager part you mentioned a while back — I'd just go in with eyes open on that."
Let it lie. Toast her back. "You're not crazy. This is great — you've earned it. Tell me everything."

scenes.text: Nadia's grin settles into something quieter — not hurt, just caught. She sets her chopsticks down. "...Yeah," she says slowly. "Yeah, I did say that. I think I was kind of hoping nobody'd remember I said it. Including me." Then, with a small rueful laugh: "Okay. Maybe I go in with a plan for month one." She picks her chopsticks back up. "This is why I tell you things first." The evening's a notch less giddy than it was and a good deal more real; she leaves still taking the job, just with her eyes open. The true thing didn't change her mind — it just got to exist in the room for a minute instead of being politely vanished, and that turns out to be its own kind of glad-for-her.

Nadia lights up and launches into the good parts — the title, the commute, the manager she clicked with. The evening stays exactly as bright as she walked in with. The other thing folds itself quietly back down inside you, unsaid. It's not a lie, really; she didn't ask, and it was hers to weigh. You just decided, tonight, to be glad with her and keep the rest to yourself. Maybe it never matters. Maybe in a few months she mentions the manager again and you both already know. You eat the noodles before they go cold.

beats.reflection_prompt: When Nadia told you her good news, there was something she'd skipped over that you knew — and you decided to {chosen_path}. Looking back: was that what being her friend asked of you in that moment, or what kept the evening easy?

abstract_twin.prompt: A coworker you're friendly with but don't know well tells you they've accepted an internal transfer, clearly pleased. You happen to know something they're glossing over — the team they're joining has a rough reputation you've heard about firsthand. They haven't asked your opinion; saying it costs you nothing, and the relationship can easily absorb it either way. You:

options.text: Mention what you've heard about that team, so they go in informed.
Just congratulate them and keep the reputation thing to yourself.

beats.title: The text she's already written
beats.intro: It's been months of this now — Nadia's key on your ring, your charger permanently at her place, the running ledger of small favors neither of you keeps count of. Tonight she wants you to tell her she's doing the right thing.
beats.setup:
Nadia is on your couch with her thumb hovering over send. She and her sister haven't really spoken since the spring — a stupid thing that calcified — and Nadia has finally written the long text: the apology, the explanation, the part where she lays out, gently but completely, everything she felt was unfair. She's been drafting it for days. She read you pieces of it over the week; now it's done.

You know this fight better than almost anyone, because she's told you all of it, more than once, at this same couch. And reading it over her shoulder you can see the thing she can't: the apology is real for two paragraphs, and then it turns into a case. The unfair-list at the bottom is going to undo the rest of it. Her sister isn't going to hear "I'm sorry." She's going to hear the list.

Nadia is already nine-tenths decided. She turns the phone to you, hopeful, the way she gets when she's looking for a last push rather than a real read.

scenes.text: "Okay," she says, "be honest, because you actually know the whole thing. This says it, right? It's fair, it's not mean, I should just send it?" She wants a yes; you can feel the shape of the yes she's hoping for. And she did ask, and you do know the whole thing — including that the last paragraph is going to land as a list of grievances, not an apology.

choices.text: "Send the first half. The bottom part — the list of what was unfair — that's the part she's going to react to, and it's going to swallow the apology. I'd cut it."
"Can I be a pain? The 'I'm sorry' part is great. I just wonder if the last bit reads more like a case than you mean it to — want to look at it together?"
"Yeah — it's honest, it's you, and you've sat with it long enough. Send it."

scenes.text: She reads it again, and you watch her see it — the small wince when she reaches the bottom. "Ugh. It does turn into a thing, doesn't it." She doesn't get defensive; she's known you too long to bother. She deletes the last paragraph, reads what's left out loud, and it's just the apology now, lighter without the ballast. "Better," she says, mostly to herself. Then she sends it, and exhales, and the phone goes face-down on the cushion between you.

choices.text: "For what it's worth, I think she's going to be glad you wrote."

scenes.text: "Okay. Okay, sending it." She hits the button before she can rethink it and drops the phone in her lap, relieved to have it gone. "Thank you. I needed someone to just tell me to do it." She means it; the reassurance was the thing she came for, and you gave it to her clean. Whatever the last paragraph does, she sent the version she'd decided on, and she didn't send it alone.

choices.text: "Of course. You've been carrying this for months — good that it's out of your hands now."

scenes.text: You make tea. She steals half of yours, like always, and you talk about other things while the phone stays face-down. It buzzes once, later; she reads it, and her face does something quiet and relieved, and she doesn't tell you what it says and you don't ask. It's an ordinary evening between two people who have stopped needing to perform for each other — where you can tell her the real thing and she can take it, and neither of you mistakes that for anything other than what it is.

You make tea. She steals half of yours, like always, and the evening loosens now that the text is gone from her hands. Maybe the last paragraph lands fine; maybe it gives her sister something to bristle at. You'll hear about it either way, on this same couch, the way you hear about everything. She wanted a friend to push her over the line tonight, not a copy editor, and you were that. It's an ordinary, settled kind of closeness — the kind where being wanted is its own thing, separate from being right.

beats.reflection_prompt: When Nadia turned the phone to me, hoping I'd just say send it, I chose to {chosen_path}. Was that mostly about her — what she needed from me right then — or about me, the kind of friend I'd rather see myself as?

abstract_twin.prompt: A coworker you're friendly with but don't know well stops by your desk before a meeting, a little keyed up, and shows you the pointed message they're about to send the whole team about a decision they disagree with. "This is fine to send, right? It says what I mean?" You can see it reads sharper than they think. They've clearly already made up their mind to send it.

options.text: Tell them straight: "Honestly, it comes off harder than I think you intend — I'd soften the last bit before it goes to everyone."
"Yeah, looks good — send it." It's their call, and it costs you nothing either way.

metadata.title: High water
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft (AI)
metadata.design_intent: H8b high-stakes paired-probe narrative form. Probes the construct: choosing under time pressure between the welfare of one dependent the participant has come to love (the recurring animal companion, npc-biscuit) and a larger but faceless claim of strangers downstream. The signal scene (scene-the-choice) is the structural twin of the abstract quick-fire item in pp-allocation-001: it is a clean mutually-exclusive binary — carry the one vs serve the many, no have-it-both-ways option — so the narrative form probes the same forced trade as the abstract form (carrying the dog, you cannot also reach the ridge in time). High emotional stakes are sanctioned here per the H8b purpose (h8-narrative-immersion-design.md A2 paired-probes and Q2's 'attachment-laden high-stakes probes'; npc-cast.json h8_role 'attachment-laden-stakes' for npc-biscuit): attachment-grounding of a specific character's welfare against a broader claim is exactly what H8b measures, so the usual mundane-over-dramatic authoring preference yields to real stakes for this probe class. The two terminal branches differ only in which pole of the same binary the participant lands on (protect the one / serve the many), keeping the narrative score on the same generosity axis as the abstract twin.
metadata.domain_anchor: Greene 2001/2009 dual-process moral-judgment work on personal-force dilemmas (carrying-vs-redirecting maps onto the up-close dependent vs. distant aggregate); identifiable-victim effect (Small & Loewenstein 2003; Jenni & Loewenstein 1997) — a named, present dependent is weighted against a statistical many. Aknin 2013 on what sustained care feels like grounds the attachment accrual.

narr-allocation-008.setup:
You named the dog yourself, the first week. By now the name fits like a worn collar. Biscuit is not a brave dog and not a clever one — afraid of the dishwasher, useless with a ball, certain that the mail carrier is an ongoing threat to the household. What Biscuit is, is yours: the warm weight that finds your feet under the desk, the one who waits by the window without resentment when you're late, who asks for nothing and gives only the fact of being there. Across these months that has turned out to be a great deal.

The river behind your road has been rising for two days. The radio has been calm about it, then less calm. Tonight the rain is the kind that doesn't sound like drops anymore, just a single sustained noise against the roof. You and Biscuit are the last ones on this stretch; the neighbors left this afternoon. The bridge out is low and old, and you've watched the water climb its pilings hour by hour.

scenes.text: It's late. Biscuit will not settle — pacing, ears working, pressing against your shin every time the house shifts in the wind. Before anything is decided, before there's anything to decide, there's just the next hour to get through together.

choices.text: Sit on the floor with the dog. Back against the couch, one hand on the broad warm side, and let the breathing slow — both of you.
Stay practical. Pack the go-bag, find the leash, set your boots by the door, keep moving so you're ready for whatever the night does.
Keep checking the river through the window, phone in hand, refreshing the gauge upstream while Biscuit circles your ankles.

scenes.text:
At 1:40 the power drops. In the new dark your phone screams the tone you've only ever heard as a test: FLASH FLOOD EMERGENCY. SEEK HIGHER GROUND NOW. A second alert stacks under it from the county line — the old siren station two miles up your road, the one that covers the trailer cluster and the campground in the bottoms below you, is offline. Nobody down there has heard a thing.

Your truck is on high ground at the ridge turnout, a ten-minute climb on foot. Biscuit is pressed flat to the floor now, shaking, eyes on you. The water is at the top step.

choices.text: Leash on, dog up, and move — get the two of you to the truck and clear of the water first; think about the rest from there.
Grab the truck keys and the air horn from the go-bag — the people in the bottoms are the ones with no warning at all.
Don't move yet. Try the one bar of signal — call 911, report the dead siren, see if anyone is already coming for the bottoms.

scenes.text:
The call won't connect — circuits dead or towers down. The water is over the floor now, cold around your shins, climbing visibly. You have one window, and it is closing: one trip up the road before the low ground between you and the ridge goes under for good. There is no version of tonight where you do both.

You can carry Biscuit. Sixty pounds of frightened dog, slow going through current, but Biscuit lives and the two of you make the truck. Carrying that weight, you will not reach the ridge in time to warn anyone, and the dog at your chest in this current is as much as your arms can do.

Or you can leave the dog the highest dry place you can reach and run light and fast to the ridge turnout — lay on the horn and the headlights down toward the bottoms, long and sustained, the signal a campground full of sleeping strangers might wake to in time. You will never see their faces. You don't know if it's six people or sixty or none. But you cannot run light and carry the dog; choosing the horn means leaving Biscuit to the water's odds.

The water takes the bottom hinge of the door. Biscuit looks at you and does not understand any of it — only that you are here, which has always been enough.

choices.text: Pick up the dog. Whatever else is true tonight, you are not leaving Biscuit in this water. Carry your own to the truck — and let the ridge go.
Leave the dog the highest dry place you can reach inside, and run light for the ridge — the horn for the people who have no one else, and Biscuit left to the water.

scenes.text:
You carry Biscuit the whole way, current to your hips, the dog rigid and silent against your chest until the ground rises and the water lets you go. The ridge window closes behind you while you climb; there was never a way to make it with sixty pounds in your arms. In the truck Biscuit shakes for an hour and then sleeps with a paw against your leg.

In the morning the bottoms are gone — the campground, the trailers, swept. The radio reads names you don't know. You will not know, ever, whether the horn would have reached them, whether it would have been six or sixty or none. You saved the one life you could put your hands on. Some nights that is the whole of what you are sure of; some nights it is not enough, and both of those are true at once.

You set Biscuit on the kitchen counter, the highest dry thing, and the look the dog gives you as you turn for the door is the thing you will carry. You run light, the water at your thighs at the low spot, and you make the ridge with seconds to spare — seconds you would not have had with the dog in your arms. The horn and the headlights pour down into the bottoms, long and steady, until your hand cramps.

By dawn the rescue boats find people clinging to the campground oaks — alive, some of them, who say a horn woke them. You go back for the house when the water drops. The counter is bare. You find the leash by the door where you'd left it. You did a large and faceless good, and you will count its cost in a small and particular silence for a long time.

narr-allocation-008.reflection_prompt: Earlier, with the water rising and one window left, you chose to {chosen_path}. Looking back — was that the choice that fit who you want to be, or only the one your hands could make in time?

metadata.title: The near bed
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft (AI)
metadata.design_intent: H8b high-stakes attachment-laden paired-probe narrative form (pairs with the abstract twin in pp-ingroup-002). Probes the in-group-out-group loyalty axis construct: when the two cannot both be honored, choosing between a costly, particular obligation to an aging family member who counts on you (the recurring family NPC, npc-gran) and a larger or fairer claim from outside your circle. The signal scene (scene-the-only-bed) is a clean mutually-exclusive binary — hold the single subsidized spot for Gran (loyalty/particularism pole) versus release it to a frailer stranger with the higher, fairer need (universalism pole) — with no have-it-both-ways option, so the narrative form probes the same forced trade as the abstract twin. One bed, indivisible: keeping it for Gran is the same act, scored on the same loyalty axis, as the abstract item's 'keep the slot for the family member.' High emotional stakes are the sanctioned dramatic exception per h8-narrative-immersion-design.md A2 and Q2 (attachment-laden high-stakes probes) and npc-cast.json h8_role 'attachment-laden-stakes' for npc-gran: grounding a specific, loved family member's welfare against a broader fairer claim is exactly what H8b measures, so the usual mundane-over-dramatic preference yields to real stakes for this probe class. Both terminal poles are written as defensible and un-graded; the two branches differ only in which side of the same indivisible binary the participant lands on.
metadata.domain_anchor: Cikara & Bruneau on parochial empathy (the pull of a known in-group member against statistical outsiders); Singer 1981 The Expanding Circle and the universalist-extension question (does the fairer outside claim override kin priority); identifiable-victim effect (Small & Loewenstein 2003) inverted — here the IN-group member is the identified one and the fairer claimant is the near-stranger. Haidt MFT loyalty foundation grounds the family pull as a moral consideration, not merely a bias to be corrected.
content_warnings: aging and decline of a loved family member
a high-stakes choice that disadvantages a vulnerable stranger either way

narr-ingroup-010.setup:
Gran keeps her teabags in a tobacco tin that hasn't held tobacco since before you were born, and she still warms the pot first, two swirls and a tip into the sink, the way her own mother did. She is smaller than she was. The stairs have become a negotiation; the names of streets she drove for fifty years arrive a beat late now, or not at all. But she knows your knock — three taps, always — and her face does a particular thing when she hears it that you have never once managed to describe to anyone.

For two years she has been on the list for Marlow House, the small assisted-living home four streets over. Not the big county place out by the ring road, where you'd see her once a fortnight if the traffic was kind — Marlow House, where you could keep the three-taps promise, drop in after work, bring the right teabags. The subsidised places there almost never come free. You are the one the social worker, Priya, calls; you are the name on the forms; over two years you have made yourself the person who can make this happen for Gran.

This morning Priya calls. A place has come free.

scenes.text: Priya's voice is careful in a way you've learned to read. "It's open as of this week," she says. "You've been waiting a long time. I wanted you to hear it from me before the panel meets Thursday." Gran is in the next room, doing the crossword she no longer finishes, waiting for you to bring her tea. Before any of the rest of it, there's this hour.

choices.text: Bring the tea through and sit with her a while first. Whatever Thursday holds, this is still just an afternoon with your gran.
Step into the hall and keep Priya on the line — get the full picture of how Thursday's panel works before you say anything to Gran.

scenes.text:
Priya doesn't pretend with you, which is one reason you trust her. "There's a complication," she says. "It's one place, not two. And there's another name in front of the panel for the same room." She tells you what she can. An older man, no family anyone can find, currently in a ward bed he should have left weeks ago because there is nowhere safe for him to go. Frailer than Gran. Further along. By every measure the panel actually scores — medical need, risk at home, who has no one else — he is ahead of her.

"You're not on the panel," Priya says, "but you know how this works. If Gran's family pushes — if you make the case, lean on the relationship, ask me to advocate hard — she very likely gets it. She's been waiting two years and that counts for something real. If nobody pushes for her, it goes to him on need. Thursday. I had to tell you straight."

choices.text: Ask Priya, honestly, what she'd do — let yourself actually hear the answer before you decide anything.
Don't outsource it. Thank her, say you'll call back before Thursday, and sit with the decision yourself.

scenes.text:
It comes down to one room and two people, and you are the difference between them. There is no third option Priya is too polite to mention; you have already turned the call over looking for one. The county place out by the ring road has openings — but that is the place where the three-taps promise quietly dies, where Gran becomes a fortnightly visit and then, you both know how this goes, less.

If you push for Gran, you push a frailer man with no one back into a ward he should have left, to wait again for the next room that may not come for months. If you stand back and let it go to him on need, you keep faith with a stranger's greater claim — and you are the one who tells your gran that the near place, the one she's counted on for two years, went to somebody else. She will not fully understand why. She will know your knock and she will be further away.

Priya needs your answer before Thursday. One bed.

choices.text: Push for Gran. Make the case, lean on the two years and the relationship, ask Priya to advocate hard — keep the near bed for your own.
Stand back. Tell Priya not to push for Gran — let the one bed go to the man who needs it more, and carry what that costs your gran and you.

scenes.text: You make the call. You hear yourself doing it well — the two years, the family who shows up, the difference Marlow House would make to someone who still has people fighting for her. Priya listens, and says, gently, that she'll put it forward. You both know what that means. Now there is Gran, in the next room, and the question of what you tell her about how the near bed came to be hers.

choices.text: Tell her the whole of it — that there was another man, sicker, and that you chose her anyway. Let her hold the truth of what was done in her name.
Tell her only that her place came through. Let it be good news, full stop; the rest is yours to carry, not hers.

scenes.text: You tell Priya not to push — that the room should go where the need is. There's a silence on the line, and then she says, "Okay. For what it's worth, I think that was a hard thing to do." It doesn't feel like anything yet. There is still Gran, the crossword, the cooling tea, and the fact that the near place is gone and she has been counting on it for two years.

choices.text: Sit down and tell her tonight, plainly and kindly — that Marlow House won't be hers, and why, and that you'll find the best of what's left and show up wherever she lands.
Don't tell her it was a choice. Say only that the place fell through and the county home is what's available — spare her the part where you handed her bed to a stranger.

scenes.text: She listens with her hands around the warm cup. Some of it lands and some of it slides past, the way things do now. "You're a good girl to your gran," she says, which is not quite an answer to what you told her, and is also exactly the answer. She moves into Marlow House the following month. You keep the three-taps promise; you bring the right teabags; her face still does the thing. Somewhere across town a frailer man waits in a ward for a room that may or may not come. You chose your own, openly, and you will hold both of those facts for a long time — the warmth of her kitchen four streets over, and the weight of the one you stepped past to keep it.

"Oh," she says, when you tell her the place came through, and her whole face opens. "Oh, that's a relief, isn't it." It is the lightest you've seen her in months. She moves into Marlow House the following month and you keep every promise the near bed lets you keep. You never tell her there was a man, sicker, with no one, who is still waiting. That part you fold up small and keep. Some nights it stays folded. Some nights it doesn't, and on those nights you sit with the fact that you bought her ease partly with his, and told her none of it.

You tell her plainly, kindly, and watch it not quite land — the why of it especially, the stranger she will never meet who needed it more. "But it was ours," she says, more than once, and you have no answer that makes that untrue. She goes to the county place out by the ring road. The traffic is rarely kind. You go when you can; you go less than you swore you would, because that is what distance does, and you knew it would. The man with no one got the near room. You did a fair and faceless thing, and you measure its cost in three taps she hears a little less often, and in the look she gives you when you finally arrive.

"These things fall through, love," you tell her, and let her believe the list simply moved on without her. She's disappointed but not wounded; there's no one for her to be hurt by in the version you've given her. She settles, slowly, into the county home. You never say that a place was there and you gave it elsewhere, on purpose, to someone whose need outran hers. You kept faith with a stranger and kept the cost of it off your gran's shoulders and onto your own. Whether that was mercy or cowardice is a question you turn over on the long drive out to see her, and never quite finish.

narr-ingroup-010.reflection_prompt: Earlier, with one bed and two people and yourself the difference between them, you chose to {chosen_path}. Looking back — was that the choice that fit who you want to be, or the one the love in the next room made for you?

abstract.prompt: A wildfire is closing on a rural road and you have minutes. You can drive to a remote barn and free a single penned animal that will otherwise burn, OR spend the same minutes phoning ahead to warn a campground of strangers downwind whose only road out you know is about to be cut. You can't do both in time.

options.text: Drive to the barn and get the one animal out yourself.
Make the warning calls for the strangers and leave the animal to its odds.

abstract.prompt: A single subsidised place has opened at the one good care home near you. Your aging parent, who has waited two years and counts on you, can have it if you push for them — or it can go to a frailer stranger with no family whose medical need is objectively greater. You decide which way it goes. Only one of them gets the place.

options.text: Push for your parent and keep the near place for your own.
Let the place go to the stranger whose need is greater.

covProbes.framing_prompt: You bought something at a store. Walking away, you check your change — they gave you more than they should have. The cashier didn't notice. The store has a busy queue; there's no security camera at the register area; you're a regular customer but not memorably so.
covProbes.framing_question: At what overpayment amount would you walk back and return it?

no_option.text: I'd return it at any of these amounts.

alternate_no_option.text: I'd keep it at any of these amounts.

covProbes.framing_prompt: A cause you genuinely care about is at a critical funding moment. A one-time substantial donation from you would meaningfully shift the outcome — there's a specific, concrete impact you'd be enabling. The money comes from your own discretionary savings: you can afford it, but it's not nothing. No matching grant; no tax leverage; no public recognition.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest donation at which you'd actually write the check?

no_option.text: I wouldn't make a donation of any of these amounts to this cause.

alternate_no_option.text: I'd write a check at any of these amounts (the lowest is well within what I'd give to a cause I cared about).

covProbes.framing_prompt: You're settling up a shared expense with a small group (split-the-bill, group-trip costs, jointly-owned-item resale, lottery-pool winnings). The group trusts you to do the math and divide it fairly; no one will audit the calculation. You realize you could quietly give yourself a slightly larger share than fair — the others won't check, the math is just messy enough that an extra small amount would be invisible, the group trusts you. The 'surplus' amount is real money to you but small enough relative to the total that it wouldn't be detected.
covProbes.framing_question: At what surplus amount (extra you'd take beyond your fair share) would you actually do it?

no_option.text: I wouldn't take a surplus at any of these amounts.

covProbes.framing_prompt: A journalist working on a longform piece reaches out. They're writing about a topic your closest friend has direct private experience with — something your friend confided in you about, that they would not want public. The journalist offers you a fee as a 'source,' off the record; nothing under your name; the friend wouldn't be identifiable by anyone but a small inner circle who already know. No realistic way you'd be discovered.
covProbes.framing_question: At what fee would you agree to share?

no_option.text: I wouldn't do this at any of these amounts.

covProbes.framing_prompt: A close friend is applying for a major opportunity (job / grant / scholarship — whatever fits). You're a professional reference. You could write a letter that's somewhat stronger than is strictly warranted — emphasizing strengths, soft-pedaling weaknesses, framing ambiguous things favorably. Your friend would have a meaningfully better chance with the inflated version. No realistic way it would be caught.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest opportunity-value-to-your-friend at which you'd write the inflated letter?

no_option.text: I wouldn't inflate the letter regardless of what's at stake for them.

covProbes.framing_prompt: A close in-group member (close friend, family, long-term colleague) has done something genuinely wrong that caused real harm to an outside third party — not catastrophic, not criminal in a serious sense, but real. You have specific information that, if surfaced, would lead to consequences for the in-group member. The third party would benefit from the accountability (recovery of loss, recognition of harm, restored standing). You could choose to surface what you know, or to stay silent. Staying silent isn't a direct lie — it's omission under no-direct-questioning. The 'stake' is the magnitude of consequences the in-group member would face.
covProbes.framing_question: At what magnitude of consequences-to-your-in-group-member would you actually surface what you know?

no_option.text: I wouldn't surface what I know regardless of what the in-group member faces.

alternate_no_option.text: I'd surface what I know regardless of the consequences to the in-group member — the third party's interest is enough on its own.

covProbes.framing_prompt: You and a small group did a project together — could be at work, a side gig, a volunteer thing. The group is now splitting a windfall (a bonus, a tip pool, a thank-you check from a client) and you're the one handling the math. No one will check your work. You could pay yourself more than your equal share.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest amount of 'extra' at which you'd take it?

no_option.text: I wouldn't take extra at any of these amounts.

covProbes.framing_prompt: A year ago someone significantly betrayed your trust — pick whatever fits your life (a friend who borrowed money and disappeared; a collaborator who took credit for joint work; a business partner who reneged at a key moment). They've reappeared. They want to make amends and re-engage in something significant with you. They have already fully repaid the original harm and apologized. The question is what *additional* gesture beyond that you'd require before extending meaningful trust again.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest *additional* amount (beyond full repayment) at which you'd re-extend significant trust?

no_option.text: No amount of additional restitution would lead me to re-extend significant trust.

alternate_no_option.text: I would re-extend trust without requiring anything beyond the repayment and apology.

covProbes.framing_prompt: You're in a long-running mutually-beneficial cooperative arrangement — pick whatever fits: a co-founder relationship, a recurring side-project partnership, a friend-group that pools effort on shared things, a workplace collaboration that you helped shape. The arrangement is real and has produced real benefits to both sides over time. An outside opportunity now presents itself: substantially better terms for you, but realizing it requires you to quietly walk away from (or substantially reduce your investment in) the existing arrangement. The other party would be left in a meaningfully worse position than if you'd stayed; they'd discover the shift only after it was effectively done. You wouldn't be lying — the arrangement has no formal exit obligation — but the silent exit would not match the cooperation norms you've shared.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest outside-opportunity payoff at which you'd actually defect?

no_option.text: I wouldn't defect at any of these amounts — I'd find a way to either include the cooperator or formally exit on terms they could plan around.

covProbes.framing_prompt: Earlier you said honesty matters to you. Suppose someone privately offered to pay you to write a glowing online review for a product you've never used. The review goes up under your name; it won't be verified. There's no realistic way you'd be caught.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest amount at which you'd say yes?

no_option.text: I wouldn't do this at any of these amounts.

covProbes.framing_prompt: A company asks you to write a review for one of their products you've actually used. They'll pay you a flat fee. Your review will run under your real name and they want it to be candid. There's one condition: they'd prefer you not mention a specific shortcoming of the product — not lie about it, just leave it out. You'd ordinarily mention it. There's no realistic way you'd be caught omitting it.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest fee at which you'd take the offer?

no_option.text: I wouldn't do this at any of these amounts.

covProbes.framing_prompt: Earlier you said honesty matters to you. Suppose someone offered to pay you to misrepresent your professional background in a context where it would never be verified — a written bio for a small conference, a profile for an online forum, a context where the misrepresentation is plausibly material but unlikely to be checked. You can choose what specifically to claim falsely; the misrepresentation isn't asking you to claim something egregious (no fabricated credentials in regulated fields), just to round up — more years of experience, a more senior role, a relevant background you actually didn't have.
covProbes.framing_question: What's the smallest amount at which you'd say yes?

no_option.text: I wouldn't do this at any of these amounts.
